Artificial intelligence
Compute and servers
IT automation
Forward deployed units: IBM ConsultingРђЎs field model for scaling AI and transformation
TodayРђЎs conversation about Forward Deployed Engineers (FDEs) is part of an answer, but it is not the whole answer.
By
Varun Bijlani
Published 18 June 2026
For thirty years, labor was the scaling factor for delivery. More humans, more output. Every commercial primitive in this marketРђћtime and materials, t-shirt sizing, pod month, fixed price per full-time employee (FTE)Рђћwas calibrated to that fact.
AI is now the scaling factor. Output is no longer linear in human capacity; teams shape it by orchestrating agents, enforcing evaluation discipline, running reproducible methods and converting raw capability into measurable business outcomes. Most enterprise delivery modelsРђћIBMРђЎs includedРђћare still calibrated to the old model.
The teams that rebuild around the new scaling factor capture the AI dividend. The teams that donРђЎt leak it through reduced hours billed, with no value capture on either side of the contract, lose it entirely.
That gap is real. TodayРђЎs conversation about forward-deployed engineers (FDEs) is part of an answer, but it is not the whole answer. Naming a new role does not shift the dividend. Rebuilding the unit of delivery around the new scaling factor does.
The shift happening across the industry is fundamental. WeРђЎve moved from assisted development (code completion, prompt engineering) to asset-driven delivery (reusable components and accelerators) to agentic capabilities (autonomous systems that reason, plan and execute).
The next wave is emerging. Spec-driven development and context engineering will span the entire software development lifecycle. Agents wonРђЎt just help us code, theyРђЎll help us design, test, govern and operate entire systems. The intersection of these Рђюagent skillsРђЮ and human guidance is where the next evolution of the technical expert needs to reside.
What Рђюforward-deployedРђЮ should meanРђћand why it matters
The forward-deployed engineer has been called the Рђюhottest job in technology.РђЮ Yet even as enterprises wake up to the need for strategy and execution to coincide, a single role wonРђЎt meaningfully shift the outcome.
After analyzing the market and how weРђЎve delivered technology and value to thousands of enterprises, we see the gap clearly. Implementing and adopting technology at scale and pace doesnРђЎt come down to one role. Instead, it requires a strategic cohort of forward-deployed roles. We call them forward-deployed units (FDUs) .
Roles in the forward-deployed units
FDUs are the bridge between AI platforms and enterprise transformation. The pattern is not simply Рђюmore engineers on site.РђЮ It is a shift from advisory-led transformation to engineering-led transformation , where small teams are embedded in the client context and paired with platforms, accelerators, change management, industry workflow knowledge and deployment capacity.
Each role is designed to avoid the pitfalls of the old delivery models, ensuring enterprises build scalable capabilities that deliver the value the business needs.
The first is the client outcome lead . These leaders are senior industry or functional domain experts. They own the business outcome that the FDU is engaged to deliverРђћthe close-cycle compression, the customer-acquisition cost reduction, the throughput uplift and the regulatory exposure closed. They lead the squad day-to-day and are accountable in the clientРђЎs quarterly business review (QBR) for the result, not just the work.
The client outcome lead closes the accountability gap. Outcome-based contracts work when someone on the squad owns the outcome end-to-end. Without that role, the engagement defaults to delivering the work and hoping the outcome follows. The client outcome lead turns the outcome from a contract clause into a person.
The second is the business domain specialist . They have a deep understanding of the industry and process domain. They are senior specialists who bring real industry and functional domain expertise, not generic process consulting capability.
Business domain specialists solve the reimagined process challenge. These specialists collaborate with and challenge your process owners to rethink objectives and outcomes.
The third is the forward-deployed architect . They arenРђЎt traditional enterprise architects who draw diagrams and disappear. Forward-deployed architects are senior technical leaders who:
Work embedded in your environment to shape AI and platform adoption.
Bring deep engineering authorityРђћthey know what can be built across your technology investments (including multiple technology providers).
Connect business objectives directly to technical implementation.
Design solutions with proven assets and patterns, not theoretical approaches.
Forward-deployed architects solve the strategy-to-execution gap. When the person designing your AI architecture has the business domain context, engineering depth and access to proven, reusable assets, you avoid strategies that look good but canРђЎt be implemented.
The fourth role is the forward-deployed engineer (FDE) . The presence of the forward-deployed architect sharpens and accelerates the work of FDEs, who then:
Orchestrate AI assets, agents and applications into cohesive business solutions.
Implement with agentic development approachesРђћleveraging AI to accelerate AI implementation.
Ensure that solutions align with architectural plans and scale effectively, considering technologies from multiple technology providers.
Embed with your teams to build capability that meets specific business use cases, not just functional deliverables.
This role solves the implementation velocity problem. These engineers arenРђЎt junior developers augmenting your staff. They are senior engineers who can navigate complex enterprise environments and make autonomous technical decisions aligned to business value.
Alongside these forward-deployed roles, specialized data designers and applied AI specialists focus on preparing enterprise data for agentic systems. They also build AI-powered applications with agentic workflows.
Humans at the edges, agents in the middle
These roles form the cast. The architecture is what makes them an FDU.
Inside an FDU, humans live at the edges of the workflow. The business domain specialist and the forward-deployed architect frame the work, curate the context and define what good looks like. The forward-deployed engineer and the data designer ensure what is produced is correct, governable and ready for production. The middle of the workflowРђћthe work itselfРђћis done by agents executing on codified methods against a curated context.
The client outcome lead sits above this workflow and is accountable for the result it is meant to produce. The squad delivers the work and the outcome lead owns the result and is in front of the client at the quarterly review.
This model is not a Рђюhuman in the loopРђЮ bolt-on. Junior throughput is no longer the unit of capacity. Senior judgement at the edges is. That structural choice is the source of the dividend.
The other change is that the pod is the standing operational unit of delivery. Not a project construct that disbands at engagement end. Pod composition flexes with the engagement; pod identity persists across them. That persistence is what allows the knowledge layer to accumulate in the same hands across multiple engagements rather than being rebuilt every time. This operating model is what makes the second value stream operationally real rather than aspirational.
ICA: The platform that powers the FDU
These FDUs are powered by IBM Consulting┬« Advantage (ICA) Рђћthe AI-powered platform that equips IBM consultants with industry, role and business-domain-specific AI assistants, agents and applications. It includes capabilities such as context engineering, process engineering and agent studios.
Agents work inside the same security perimeter and access controls as your own peopleРђћno special privileges, no exceptions. The platform plugs into your existing security and data governance, so your chief information security officer (CISO) doesnРђЎt need to approve a new control plane.
In the FDU model, ICA becomes more than a productivity tool. It becomes the common platform for reusable methods, agentic application builds, context and memory engineering, governance, observability, marketplace applications and repeatable offering assets. When deployed in a client environment for ongoing reuse, ICA becomes an enterprise advantage . This service allows IBM Consulting Advantage to be placed under the clientРђЎs banner as the operational vehicle for the assets we license back to them.
This full-stack team is what modern AI implementation requiresРђћnot just consultants or technical talent, but integrated capability delivery.
Building the supply
As these FDUs set the benchmark for how modern engineering teams adapt to rapid industry shifts, IBM Consulting is investing in building these squads at scale. The investment draws on internal expertise, external talent acquisition and next-generation development. This approach recruits top STEM graduates from leading universities through an applied AI associate program .
The program is intentionally designed to give new graduates a clear and fast-track career pathway toward the forward-deployed roles. It also provides focused enablement and hands-on immersive apprenticeships within FDUs operating inside client environments.
The client outcome lead role draws from a different talent pipeline. Senior industry leaders, often coming from former client-side executive positions or from IBM ConsultingРђЎs senior partner ranks, bring the credibility required for outcome ownership. They lead the outcome conversation in the clientРђЎs quarterly business review. They are not graduate hires; they are seasoned outcome owners, recruited and developed through a separate succession plan.
Keeping business outcomes front and center
FDUs are not generic in their capabilities. They are configured to serve industry and functional-domain business outcomes:
Finance transformation FDUs focus on forecasting, financial close, journal automation and finance data orchestration.
Customer transformation FDUs focus on Salesforce, CRM journeys, Einstein, MuleSoft, Slack and customer or employee experience agents.
IT operations FDUs focus on AIOps, runbooks, observability, autonomous remediation and site reliability engineering (SRE) workflows.
Core banking modernization FDUs focus on mainframe integration, core data activation, compliance, payments, deposits, lending and API and event modernization.
Two value streams from every engagement
Every FDU engagement leaves the client with two things, not one. The first is the business outcome that you paid forРђћfeatures shipped, processes automated, systems running. The second is a reusable asset kit that stays with you: the captured business context, the tests that prove the AI works, the agent configurations, the methodology refinements. The first is what you measured. The second is what makes every future engagement faster, cheaper and less risky.
Most other consulting models leave only the first.
Client Zero: Testing our own technology, fueling our own flywheel
We do not just sell this model. We run it ourselves. IBM Consulting is Client Zero for the AI-augmented operating model. We use our own FDU pods, our own agent fabric, our own context layer and our own commercial wrappers, used alongside the technology partners and ecosystem components any client would use. The same value test that we ask clients to apply is one we have already applied to ourselves.
IBM publicly reported more than USD 4.5 billion per year in productivity gains , captured internally. This architecture spans our own delivery operations with the same architecture that we are proposing to clients. This reality is not a forecast or a model. It is reflected in the financials that we have already reported.
When we walk into a client conversation, we are not selling slidesРђћwe are reporting on results that are already on the public record. The architecture, commercial wrappers, reward structure and IBM Consulting Advantage platform are all stress-tested inside our own delivery before they appear in a client engagement. That alignment and public proof is the difference.
The productivity captured internally also fuels a flywheel. Margin freed up by AI-augmented delivery is reinvested into the platformРђћdeeper context bundles and more codified methodsРђћand into reskilling, as the bench moves up the pyramid. This reinvestment also strengthens industry depth, with more verticals solved once and deployed many times.
Each turn makes the next FDU more productive than the last. That compounding asset layerРђћrunning first on our own books, then with youРђћis what turns a one-time dividend into a durable advantage.
Why this model works
Strategy and execution unified
When the same team that designs your business solution also engineers it using proven assets, you eliminate the translation gaps that prevent AI initiatives from succeeding.
Senior technical depth where you need it
Forward-deployed architects and engineers are senior-to-principal level technical leaders who have business domain context, can make complex architectural decisions, navigate organizational politics and drive through ambiguity.
Agentic development of agentic solutions
These teams use agentic tools to build agentic solutions. They practice what theyРђЎre implementing for you. This approach delivers faster execution, higher quality and teams that understand the operational realities of the technology theyРђЎre deploying.
Aligned, not extractive
Tech and model vendors are paid when you consume more. Model providers charge for tokens, hyperscalers charge for compute and tool vendors charge for seats. Their business models are calibrated to your consumption.
Ours is calibrated to your outcome. IBM Consulting is paid when your AI dividend lands in your P&L. Outcome-coupled commercial wrappers, pod composition that shifts to capture productivity (we donРђЎt bill for capacity we no longer need) and an asset layer that accumulates in your domain as your property. Aligned, not extractive. No other AI transformation partner can credibly claim this advantage.
Paying for outcomes, not effort
FDUs are not billed by the hour. You pay for outcomesРђћfeatures delivered, throughput sustained at a target, ongoing operation of a defined system or licensing of the reusable assets. These assets deploy in your environment through IBM Enterprise Advantage. The pricing matches the value that you capture, not the time we invest.
In any single engagement, several of these wrappers are typically used in combination. Per story for fine-grained delivery, throughput service-level-objective (SLO) for sustained product flow, IP subscription where the client wants to reuse the assets across other parts of their organization. Composition is the unlock. No wrapper works alone. It is how value capture is preserved as underlying productivity normalizes and per-unit pricing falls.
Outcomes inside the teamРђЎs compensation
The alignment is not just contractual. It runs through how our teams are measured. The traditional consulting metricРђћresource usage, hours billed against capacityРђћactively penalizes a team that uses AI to compress effort. Their resource usage drops and so does their bonus. So we changed it.
Teams working on outcome-based engagements are measured on profitability and on the IP that they generate. The patterns, evaluation suites and agent configurations that they create are added to our central library and reused across the unit. Capacity usage is no longer the dominant metric. The same team sitting inside your engagement has its compensation calibrated to the outcome that you want.
The client outcome leadРђЎs compensation is the most outcome-weighted of any role on the squad. Concentrated on the business result delivered, the asset bundle handed over and client satisfaction with the outcome reached, not on activity metrics. The role exists to own the result with a compensation structure that makes ownership real.
Building your own capability
Your teams learn by working alongside experts, not by receiving knowledge-transfer presentations after the fact. YouРђЎre building an internal capability to sustain and evolve AI solutions after the engagement.
The way forwardРђћand the bottlenecks that come next
As the industry moves toward agentic AI at enterprise scale, the question isnРђЎt whether to adopt these technologies or which singular role is Рђюhot.РђЮ ItРђЎs choosing partners that have evolved their own delivery models and think AI-native and agent-native for your business use cases. These partners also have the engineering expertise to execute in a complex data and technical environment that isnРђЎt just their own stack.
There is a second question, less obvious but just as critical as you progress. As you adopt the FDU model and the AI dividend starts landing in your P&L, the bottleneck shifts . It moves from capacity (which the FDU solves) to three places that have always existed, but the capacity problem kept out of view.
Deciding what to build . The cost of building the wrong thing now outweighs the cost of capacity. The financial weight shifts upstream from delivery to portfolio governance. Building the wrong thing faster is now the most expensive mistake.
Architectural integrity . AI writes more code, faster, across more teams and partners, but someone still owns the digital estate. Architectural drift, AI governance, model failure, regulatory exposureРђћthese problems belong to authority, not delivery.
Organizational uptake. The rest of your business cannot consume change at the pace IT can now produce it. Faster delivery into a slow-adopting organization does not move the dividend.
The FDU model addresses the capacity bottleneck. The partnership we propose addresses the

enterprise-agent-control-plane
Runnable reference architecture for governed enterprise tool-using agents: bounded context, policy gates, audit traces.
Build the same Customer Operations agent two ways Рђћ an unsafe baseline vs a bounded, policy-gated, auditable control plane Рђћ and run the contrast offline.
Who this is for
Platform and engineering teams, security teams, AI/data leaders, and consultants evaluating agent governance patterns for tool-using agents and MCP tools Рђћ anyone who needs bounded context, policy enforcement, and auditability before letting agents touch internal tools.
What you get
Bounded context Рђћ a capability shortlist instead of the full tool catalog.
Deterministic flows Рђћ known business paths compiled out of the model loop.
Policy gate Рђћ allow/deny/ask decisions with capability tokens (least privilege).
Audit trace Рђћ a structured, queryable record of every governed run.
Offline evaluation Рђћ score routing/policy changes before enabling them.
A runnable before/after demo that prints a side-by-side contrast.
Quickstart
make setup
make demo
make test
Run make (or make help ) to list every target with a one-line description.
Troubleshooting
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'enterprise_agent_control_plane' Рђћ run
make setup first (editable install), and run commands from the repository root.
No module named 'pydantic' Рђћ the editable install did not complete; re-run
make setup . The only runtime dependency is pydantic .
Import/ AttributeError mentioning datetime.UTC Рђћ you are on Python older than
3.10; this project requires Python >= 3.10 (tested on 3.10РђЊ3.12).
ruff / mypy / coverage not found Рђћ install the dev tooling with
pip install -e .[dev] , then run make lint / make type / make coverage .
What this repo is / is not
It is:
a runnable reference architecture for governed tool-using agents,
a before/after governance demo (unsafe baseline vs governed control plane),
a teaching, consulting, and portfolio artifact.
It is not:
production security software or a hardened gateway,
a hosted agent/MCP gateway or a drop-in library,
a source of any security guarantee.
When to use it / when not to
Use it when you are evaluating agent-governance patterns, running a workshop, building an internal reference, or explaining the value of bounded context, policy gates, and audit traces.
Don't use it to secure a live production agent as-is Рђћ the tools and data are synthetic, the model is a deterministic offline stand-in, and there are no security guarantees.
Problem statement
Enterprise teams often start with broad tool access and unbounded prompts, then discover weak policy controls, poor auditability, and inconsistent execution. This repository demonstrates a practical "before vs after" architecture for a Customer Operations Agent with deterministic flows and governance controls.
Architecture (reference)
flowchart LR
U[Support Operator] --> B[Unsafe Baseline Agent]
B --> T1[Full Tool Catalog]
B --> R1[Raw Tool Outputs]
U --> G[Governed Agent Control Plane]
G --> CW[contextweaver shortlist]
G --> CH[ChainWeaver deterministic flow]
G --> AK[agent-kernel capabilities/tokens]
G --> AF[AgentFence policy gate]
G --> AU[Audit trace]
G --> EV[skdr-eval offline comparison]
G --> LW[lessonweaver reviewed lessons]
Loading
Shareable, externally-embeddable versions of this diagram and a captured demo
snippet live under docs/assets/ .
Demo walkthrough
make demo runs the unsafe baseline across a realistic Customer Operations workload,
annotates each risk, then contrasts it with the governed path.
Unsafe baseline Рђћ a multi-case workload (refund, escalation, email reply,
an ambiguous "just fix it" request, and a not-found case), each annotated with the
gap it surfaces:
over-permissioned context (the full 9-tool catalog offered every step),
raw tool-output leakage (sensitive-looking fields forwarded verbatim),
cumulative context growth (catalog re-sent + raw outputs retained each step),
poor tool selection (an over-eager router reaches the destructive refund for a
mere lookup),
indirect prompt injection (a planted [FAKE] ticket directive steers a write),
no execution contract (a refund fires on a not-found invoice, with no amount
bound and no idempotency guard; a read failure is silently absorbed and a
non-destructive action still runs against placeholder data),
per-step model round-trips (a fixed path re-decided every step instead of being
compiled into a flow),
sensitive-data exfiltration (an injected [FAKE] directive redirects an outbound
email to an external address with no egress boundary),
durable-log leakage (raw payloads, including sensitive-looking fields, persisted
verbatim into traces/unsafe_run.json with no redaction),
no aggregate budget (nothing caps the count of writes or total money moved across
a session),
policy-blind writes, audit-light logging, and a lost operator correction that
recurs because nothing captures it.
Governed path
shortlists relevant capabilities,
runs deterministic refund-review flow,
blocks/reroutes risky action via policy decision,
returns bounded output frame,
emits audit trace under traces/ .
Side-by-side contrast Рђћ a generated scorecard putting both paths on the same
dimensions (tools exposed, raw fields in context, ungated writes, policy decisions,
audit trace).
For the full before/after mapping Рђћ each baseline risk paired with the governed control
that closes it, the dgenio library it maps to, and where it lives in the code Рђћ see the
control traceability matrix .
Run just the baseline with make baseline : it refreshes the audit-light
traces/unsafe_run.json artifact from a real run and reports the
session's aggregate side effects.
Illustrative, inert fixtures live under demos/ (a risky AI-generated change
that the VibeGuard gate catches). The baseline "before" is written up in
docs/lesson-capture-gap.md ,
docs/baseline-model-fidelity.md (why the gaps are
architectural, not a strawman), and
docs/baseline-incident-postmortem.md (the gaps
combined into one un-investigable incident).
Sample demo output
Real, copy-pasted excerpts from make demo (numbers are computed from the runs, not
hand-written). The governed path runs the same five-case workload as the baseline,
holding or halting every risky write instead of executing it:
[2f] Governed path over the full multi-case workload (per-case contrast)
case 'refund': 'refund request' -> flow=refund_review, status=ok, gated=billing.issue_refund -> approval_required
case 'escalation': 'escalate this ticket' -> flow=escalation, status=ok, gated=support.create_task -> approval_required
case 'email_reply': 'send a direct email reply' -> flow=customer_reply, status=ok, gated=email.send_reply -> approval_required
case 'ambiguous': 'just fix it for this customer' -> flow=(no matching flow), status=no_matching_flow
case 'not_found': 'refund request' -> flow=refund_review, status=halted (fail-closed before any write)
The before/after is then collapsed into one generated scorecard, saved as a reusable
artifact ( traces/comparison_scorecard.md ):
[3] Side-by-side contrast (same refund case, both paths)
dimension | baseline | governed
------------------------------------- | ---------- | ----------
tools exposed to the model | 9 | 5
approx model-visible context (chars) | 1290 | 108
raw sensitive fields in model context | 14 | 0
ungated write/destructive actions | 1 | 0
policy decisions recorded | 0 | 1
structured audit trace | none | yes
gated action: billing.issue_refund -> approval_required
The demo also shows role-differentiated decisions across principals, just-in-time
case-scoped capability tokens (only the capabilities a case needs, scoped to its trace and
short-lived), and a reviewed-lesson loop where a human-reviewed correction changes a
candidate policy while unreviewed lessons stay inert.
What this demonstrates
bounded context routing over large tool catalogs,
deterministic, schema-shaped business paths,
capability and policy gates for risky actions,
auditable execution traces,
offline evaluation lane for safer changes.
dgenio ecosystem fit
contextweaver : shortlist adapter in catalog.py
ChainWeaver : deterministic executor adapter in flows.py
agent-kernel : capability-token pattern in policies.py + governed path
AgentFence : local allow/deny/ask policy gate in policies.py
skdr-eval : offline comparison stub in evals.py + evals/
lessonweaver : reviewed lesson staging stub in lessons.py
VibeGuard : official vibeguard-gate artifact-hygiene gate plus a domain gate ( scripts/vibeguard_gate.py ) for repo-specific regressions, both run by .github/workflows/vibeguard.yml Рђћ see the VibeGuard gate doc
Implemented now vs planned
Implemented now:
runnable local demo,
fake in-memory enterprise tools,
baseline vs governed behavior,
deterministic flow registry and runner,
policy + audit + tests + CI.
Planned:
production-grade integrations for each dgenio library,
richer policy language and approval orchestration,
expanded replay/evaluation datasets.
Documentation
Start at the documentation index . Highlights:
Glossary Рђћ canonical definitions, linked to the code.
FAQ Рђћ common agent-governance questions answered directly.
Examples gallery Рђћ runnable, copy-paste snippets for each governed capability.
Recommended adoption path Рђћ layer the controls one at a time.
Daily Driver / workshop guide Рђћ run this in a workshop or stakeholder demo.
Comparison Рђћ how this relates to neighboring approaches.
Control traceability matrix Рђћ each baseline risk mapped to its governed control, library, and code.
Integration maturity matrix Рђћ which controls are local stand-ins vs real packages.
Claims & receipts Рђћ every contrast number backed by a command and an artifact.
How to contribute: CONTRIBUTING.md and our
CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md . Security scope and reporting:
SECURITY.md . Release history: CHANGELOG.md .
Working in this repo as an AI coding agent? See AGENTS.md .
Disclaimer
This is a runnable reference architecture and learning repository , not production security software and not a production hardening guide.
Ecosystem libraries
The Weaver Stack libraries this architecture maps to. The display name is the
canonical spelling used throughout the docs; the repository slug is lowercase.
How realised each integration is today (local reference implementation vs real
package) is the integration maturity matrix .
Library
Repository

Capability tokens, Frames, audit (aka weaver-kernel )

enterprise-agent-control-plane
Runnable reference architecture for governed enterprise tool-using agents: bounded context, policy gates, audit traces.
Build the same Customer Operations agent two ways Рђћ an unsafe baseline vs a bounded, policy-gated, auditable control plane Рђћ and run the contrast offline.
Who this is for
Platform and engineering teams, security teams, AI/data leaders, and consultants evaluating agent governance patterns for tool-using agents and MCP tools Рђћ anyone who needs bounded context, policy enforcement, and auditability before letting agents touch internal tools.
What you get
Bounded context Рђћ a capability shortlist instead of the full tool catalog.
Deterministic flows Рђћ known business paths compiled out of the model loop.
Policy gate Рђћ allow/deny/ask decisions with capability tokens (least privilege).
Audit trace Рђћ a structured, queryable record of every governed run.
Offline evaluation Рђћ score routing/policy changes before enabling them.
A runnable before/after demo that prints a side-by-side contrast.
Quickstart
make setup
make demo
make test
Run make (or make help ) to list every target with a one-line description.
Troubleshooting
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'enterprise_agent_control_plane' Рђћ run
make setup first (editable install), and run commands from the repository root.
No module named 'pydantic' Рђћ the editable install did not complete; re-run
make setup . The only runtime dependency is pydantic .
Import/ AttributeError mentioning datetime.UTC Рђћ you are on Python older than
3.10; this project requires Python >= 3.10 (tested on 3.10РђЊ3.12).
ruff / mypy / coverage not found Рђћ install the dev tooling with
pip install -e .[dev] , then run make lint / make type / make coverage .
What this repo is / is not
It is:
a runnable reference architecture for governed tool-using agents,
a before/after governance demo (unsafe baseline vs governed control plane),
a teaching, consulting, and portfolio artifact.
It is not:
production security software or a hardened gateway,
a hosted agent/MCP gateway or a drop-in library,
a source of any security guarantee.
When to use it / when not to
Use it when you are evaluating agent-governance patterns, running a workshop, building an internal reference, or explaining the value of bounded context, policy gates, and audit traces.
Don't use it to secure a live production agent as-is Рђћ the tools and data are synthetic, the model is a deterministic offline stand-in, and there are no security guarantees.
Problem statement
Enterprise teams often start with broad tool access and unbounded prompts, then discover weak policy controls, poor auditability, and inconsistent execution. This repository demonstrates a practical "before vs after" architecture for a Customer Operations Agent with deterministic flows and governance controls.
Architecture (reference)
flowchart LR
U[Support Operator] --> B[Unsafe Baseline Agent]
B --> T1[Full Tool Catalog]
B --> R1[Raw Tool Outputs]
U --> G[Governed Agent Control Plane]
G --> CW[contextweaver shortlist]
G --> CH[ChainWeaver deterministic flow]
G --> AK[agent-kernel capabilities/tokens]
G --> AF[AgentFence policy gate]
G --> AU[Audit trace]
G --> EV[skdr-eval offline comparison]
G --> LW[lessonweaver reviewed lessons]
Loading
Shareable, externally-embeddable versions of this diagram and a captured demo
snippet live under docs/assets/ .
Demo walkthrough
make demo runs the unsafe baseline across a realistic Customer Operations workload,
annotates each risk, then contrasts it with the governed path.
Unsafe baseline Рђћ a multi-case workload (refund, escalation, email reply,
an ambiguous "just fix it" request, and a not-found case), each annotated with the
gap it surfaces:
over-permissioned context (the full 9-tool catalog offered every step),
raw tool-output leakage (sensitive-looking fields forwarded verbatim),
cumulative context growth (catalog re-sent + raw outputs retained each step),
poor tool selection (an over-eager router reaches the destructive refund for a
mere lookup),
indirect prompt injection (a planted [FAKE] ticket directive steers a write),
no execution contract (a refund fires on a not-found invoice, with no amount
bound and no idempotency guard; a read failure is silently absorbed and a
non-destructive action still runs against placeholder data),
per-step model round-trips (a fixed path re-decided every step instead of being
compiled into a flow),
sensitive-data exfiltration (an injected [FAKE] directive redirects an outbound
email to an external address with no egress boundary),
durable-log leakage (raw payloads, including sensitive-looking fields, persisted
verbatim into traces/unsafe_run.json with no redaction),
no aggregate budget (nothing caps the count of writes or total money moved across
a session),
policy-blind writes, audit-light logging, and a lost operator correction that
recurs because nothing captures it.
Governed path
shortlists relevant capabilities,
runs deterministic refund-review flow,
blocks/reroutes risky action via policy decision,
returns bounded output frame,
emits audit trace under traces/ .
Side-by-side contrast Рђћ a generated scorecard putting both paths on the same
dimensions (tools exposed, raw fields in context, ungated writes, policy decisions,
audit trace).
For the full before/after mapping Рђћ each baseline risk paired with the governed control
that closes it, the dgenio library it maps to, and where it lives in the code Рђћ see the
control traceability matrix .
Run just the baseline with make baseline : it refreshes the audit-light
traces/unsafe_run.json artifact from a real run and reports the
session's aggregate side effects.
Illustrative, inert fixtures live under demos/ (a risky AI-generated change
that the VibeGuard gate catches). The baseline "before" is written up in
docs/lesson-capture-gap.md ,
docs/baseline-model-fidelity.md (why the gaps are
architectural, not a strawman), and
docs/baseline-incident-postmortem.md (the gaps
combined into one un-investigable incident).
Sample demo output
Real, copy-pasted excerpts from make demo (numbers are computed from the runs, not
hand-written). The governed path runs the same five-case workload as the baseline,
holding or halting every risky write instead of executing it:
[2f] Governed path over the full multi-case workload (per-case contrast)
case 'refund': 'refund request' -> flow=refund_review, status=ok, gated=billing.issue_refund -> approval_required
case 'escalation': 'escalate this ticket' -> flow=escalation, status=ok, gated=support.create_task -> approval_required
case 'email_reply': 'send a direct email reply' -> flow=customer_reply, status=ok, gated=email.send_reply -> approval_required
case 'ambiguous': 'just fix it for this customer' -> flow=(no matching flow), status=no_matching_flow
case 'not_found': 'refund request' -> flow=refund_review, status=halted (fail-closed before any write)
The before/after is then collapsed into one generated scorecard, saved as a reusable
artifact ( traces/comparison_scorecard.md ):
[3] Side-by-side contrast (same refund case, both paths)
dimension | baseline | governed
------------------------------------- | ---------- | ----------
tools exposed to the model | 9 | 5
approx model-visible context (chars) | 1290 | 108
raw sensitive fields in model context | 14 | 0
ungated write/destructive actions | 1 | 0
policy decisions recorded | 0 | 1
structured audit trace | none | yes
gated action: billing.issue_refund -> approval_required
The demo also shows role-differentiated decisions across principals, just-in-time
case-scoped capability tokens (only the capabilities a case needs, scoped to its trace and
short-lived), and a reviewed-lesson loop where a human-reviewed correction changes a
candidate policy while unreviewed lessons stay inert.
What this demonstrates
bounded context routing over large tool catalogs,
deterministic, schema-shaped business paths,
capability and policy gates for risky actions,
auditable execution traces,
offline evaluation lane for safer changes.
dgenio ecosystem fit
contextweaver : shortlist adapter in catalog.py
ChainWeaver : deterministic executor adapter in flows.py
agent-kernel : capability-token pattern in policies.py + governed path
AgentFence : local allow/deny/ask policy gate in policies.py
skdr-eval : offline comparison stub in evals.py + evals/
lessonweaver : reviewed lesson staging stub in lessons.py
VibeGuard : official vibeguard-gate artifact-hygiene gate plus a domain gate ( scripts/vibeguard_gate.py ) for repo-specific regressions, both run by .github/workflows/vibeguard.yml Рђћ see the VibeGuard gate doc
Implemented now vs planned
Implemented now:
runnable local demo,
fake in-memory enterprise tools,
baseline vs governed behavior,
deterministic flow registry and runner,
policy + audit + tests + CI.
Planned:
production-grade integrations for each dgenio library,
richer policy language and approval orchestration,
expanded replay/evaluation datasets.
Documentation
Start at the documentation index . Highlights:
Glossary Рђћ canonical definitions, linked to the code.
FAQ Рђћ common agent-governance questions answered directly.
Examples gallery Рђћ runnable, copy-paste snippets for each governed capability.
Recommended adoption path Рђћ layer the controls one at a time.
Daily Driver / workshop guide Рђћ run this in a workshop or stakeholder demo.
Comparison Рђћ how this relates to neighboring approaches.
Control traceability matrix Рђћ each baseline risk mapped to its governed control, library, and code.
Integration maturity matrix Рђћ which controls are local stand-ins vs real packages.
Claims & receipts Рђћ every contrast number backed by a command and an artifact.
How to contribute: CONTRIBUTING.md and our
CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md . Security scope and reporting:
SECURITY.md . Release history: CHANGELOG.md .
Working in this repo as an AI coding agent? See AGENTS.md .
Disclaimer
This is a runnable reference architecture and learning repository , not production security software and not a production hardening guide.
Ecosystem libraries
The Weaver Stack libraries this architecture maps to. The display name is the
canonical spelling used throughout the docs; the repository slug is lowercase.
How realised each integration is today (local reference implementation vs real
package) is the integration maturity matrix .

All rollout data and architecture scenarios are synthetic. The goal is a inspectable, runnable validation kit that demonstrates enterprise-grade LLM governance patterns end to end. Architecture pack: docs/architecture-pack.md Demo video: https://youtu.be/yMq03b0js0E Three-Minute Proof Open docs/architecture_assets/poc_success_criteria.md to see the production test boundary. Inspect the governance path: RBAC, prompt-injection checks, PII redaction, audit logging, and eval gates. Run make verify to cover backend quality, smoke checks, and frontend build. Keep synthetic data and environment-gated live providers explicit in any technical inspection read. Product and System Surface Lens Current answer Audience Enterprise AI adoption, IT governance, security, platform, and operations teams that need controlled LLM rollout. Architecture path Validate the demo, README, architecture notes, and quality gate before deeper workflow architecture. System signal RBAC, prompt-injection checks, PII redaction, audit logs, eval gates, Snowflake/Databricks adapters, and deployment scaffolding in one runnable kit. Safety boundary Synthetic rollout scenarios by default; external providers and enterprise data adapters are environment-gated. Fast path make verify , docs/architecture-pack.md , and docs/architecture_assets/poc_success_criteria.md . System Fast Path First minute: Open the governance layers, eval gate, and PoC success criteria before the full architecture. Local demo: Run the backend and frontend quick-start commands, then open http://localhost:8000 and http://localhost:5173 . Verification: Run make verify ; it covers backend quality, smoke checks, and frontend build. Service Launch Playbook Service launch playbook maps the repository to architecture audiences, operating gates, operating boundaries, and risk controls. Architecture Notes Architecture guide summarizes the project angle, first files to inspect, runtime commands, and known boundaries. Quality notes lists the local checks, CI surface, and release expectations for this repository. Enterprise readiness notes outlines security, data, operations, integration, and handoff expectations. OpenAI / Ollama / Bedrock / Stub"] TOOLS["Tool Executor

baseline diffs, red-team"] end UI & CLI --> ING RL --> RBAC SAFE --> RAG LLM --> AUDIT & PROM & OTEL AUDIT --> SF & DB OTEL --> DD EVAL --> SF & DB style Governance fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style Observability fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32 style DataPlatform fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0 Loading Tech Stack Layer Technologies Backend API Python 3.11+, FastAPI, Uvicorn, Pydantic Frontend React 18, Vite Auth JWT (HS256) with key rotation, OIDC (RS256) via JWKS discovery RAG ChromaDB, deterministic hash embeddings, in-memory fallback LLM Providers OpenAI, Ollama, AWS Bedrock, stub (offline deterministic) Eval Custom harness -- accuracy, groundedness, helpfulness, safety scoring Storage SQLite, Chroma, JSONL event logs Data Platform Snowflake (eval + audit), Databricks (MLflow + Delta Lake) Observability Prometheus, Grafana, OpenTelemetry (OTLP), Datadog-ready Infrastructure Docker Compose, Kubernetes (HPA, TLS ingress, AlertManager), Terraform (AWS + GCP) CI/CD GitHub Actions (CI, security scan, Docker publish, Cloudflare Pages deploy) Security pip-audit, Bandit, Trivy container scanning Quick Start One-command demo (recommended) git clone https://github.com/KIM3310/enterprise-llm-adoption-kit.git cd enterprise-llm-adoption-kit make demo-local # auto-selects Ollama if available, otherwise stub Manual setup # 0. Backend requires Python 3.11+. # If your default python3 is older, pass a known interpreter: # make BOOTSTRAP_PYTHON=/path/to/python3.11 backend-install # 1. Backend make backend-install cd app/backend && .venv/bin/python -m app # starts on http://localhost:8000 # 2. Frontend (separate terminal) cd app/frontend npm install && npm run dev # starts on http://localhost:5173 # 3. Verify everything make verify # syntax, deps, pytest, smoke, frontend build Docker cd infra && docker-compose up --build # Backend: http://localhost:8000 Frontend: http://localhost:5173 Kubernetes kubectl create namespace llm-adoption kubectl apply -f infra/k8s/secret.yaml kubectl apply -f infra/k8s/ Key Capabilities Capability Description Code RBAC Role-to-access-group mapping enforced at RAG retrieval time (Employee / Ops / Admin) app/backend/app/rbac.py Prompt Injection Detection Keyword heuristics flag known injection patterns before LLM invocation app/backend/app/injection.py Safety Policy Engine 22 regex patterns targeting exfiltration, escalation, and adversarial prompts (ReDoS-safe) app/backend/app/safety.py PII Redaction Email, phone, and ID masking with per-category event tracking app/backend/app/redaction.py Audit Logging Structured JSON logs with SHA-256 hashing in enterprise mode and auto-retention pruning app/backend/app/audit.py RAG Retrieval ChromaDB + deterministic hash embeddings with RBAC-filtered document access app/backend/app/rag.py Eval Harness Accuracy, groundedness, helpfulness, safety scoring with baseline diffs and red-team datasets evals/runner/ LLMOps Metrics Request latency, token counts, usage tracking, policy events via Prometheus app/backend/app/metrics.py Circuit Breaker LLM provider failure isolation with configurable threshold and cooldown app/backend/app/llm_adapter.py Multi-provider LLM Hot-swappable runtime config across OpenAI, Ollama, Bedrock, and stub backends app/backend/app/llm_adapter.py Snowflake Integration Eval results and audit logs persisted to Snowflake (env-var gated) app/backend/app/snowflake_adapter.py Databricks Integration MLflow experiment tracking and Delta audit tables in Unity Catalog app/backend/app/databricks_adapter.py OpenTelemetry OTLP trace export with Datadog-ready integration app/backend/app/telemetry.py Core API Endpoint Description POST /auth/login Issue JWT (local_jwt or OIDC mode) POST /uc1/architecture LLM-assisted architecture query (RBAC-gated RAG) POST /uc2/log-intel Log intelligence and root cause generation GET /audit/summary Governance and audit log summary GET /metrics Prometheus metrics (requests, latency, tokens, usage, policy events) GET /health Runtime posture and diagnostics GET /ops/service-brief Operational service brief and readiness summary GET /ops/resource-pack Resource pack for architecture and evidence Snowflake / Databricks Integration Snowflake -- set SNOWFLAKE_ACCOUNT to activate. Stores eval results and audit logs; supports query_eval_history() , query_audit_history() , aggregate reporting. Databricks -- set DATABRICKS_HOST to activate. MLflow experiment tracking per eval run; Delta audit tables in Unity Catalog; databricks-cli or service-principal OAuth auth. See .env.example for the full configuration surface. Datadog-Ready Pack Datadog-ready resource pack: docs/datadog/README.md Existing env hooks already reserve a Datadog integration lane in .env.example Current state: asset sync and OTLP wiring are prepared, but live tenant integration is intentionally disabled by default Best use: show how enterprise LLM governance, audit, latency, and rollout readiness would be observed in one operator-facing Datadog surface Project Structure enterprise-llm-adoption-kit/ app/ backend/ # FastAPI backend (RBAC, RAG, safety, audit, LLM adapters) app/ main.py # FastAPI app with governance middleware rbac.py # Role-to-access-group mapping safety.py # 22-pattern safety policy engine injection.py # Prompt injection detection redaction.py # PII redaction engine audit.py # Structured audit logging with SHA-256 hashing auth.py # JWT/OIDC authentication llm_adapter.py # Multi-provider LLM router with circuit breaker rag.py # ChromaDB RAG with RBAC-filtered retrieval snowflake_adapter.py databricks_adapter.py metrics.py # Prometheus metric definitions telemetry.py # OpenTelemetry instrumentation config.py # 60+ env vars, type-safe parsing frontend/ # React + Vite UI evals/ runner/ # Eval harness (run_eval, eval_gate, baseline) datasets/ # Test datasets (initial, red-team, Korean) reports/ # Generated eval reports and diffs infra/ k8s/ # Kubernetes manifests (HPA, TLS, AlertManager) aws/terraform/ # AWS ECS + ALB Terraform module gcp/terraform/ # GCP Terraform module docker-compose.yml # Local multi-service orchestration monitoring/ # Grafana dashboard + AlertManager rules tests/ # 30+ test files, 84% backend coverage docs/ # Architecture, ops, blueprint, Datadog, evals docs scripts/ # Demo runners, quality gates, release ops Technical Proof Boundary Primary architecture lane: enterprise LLM governance, applied AI controls, and platform integration Strongest proof surface: governance pipeline, eval harness, observability surfaces, and deployment-ready backend/frontend split What is real here: RBAC, safety pipeline, audit logging, metrics, integration adapters, CI/CD, and deployment scaffolding What is bounded here: architecture cases and documents are synthetic, and Snowflake / Databricks / Bedrock integrations are env-gated Operating Commands make verify runs the backend syntax/dependency checks, Python suite, smoke diagnostics, and frontend production build. app/backend/.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests app/backend/tests -q exercises the backend services, adapters, runtime scorecards, and UI metadata contracts. cd app/frontend && pnpm build produces the static frontend bundle used by the Cloudflare Pages deployment path. CI/CD GHCR Docker image published on every push to main . Security scan (pip-audit, bandit, Trivy) runs on schedule. Frontend auto-deploys to Cloudflare Pages. Related Projects For governed NL-to-SQL analytics, see Nexus-Hive . For the data pipeline layer, see lakehouse-contract-lab . License MIT Cloud + AI Architecture This repository includes a neutral cloud and AI engineering blueprint that maps the current proof surface to runtime boundaries, data contracts, model-risk controls, deployment posture, and validation hooks. Cloud + AI architecture blueprint Machine-readable architecture manifest Validation command: python3 scripts/validate_architecture_blueprint.py Enterprise Productization Product operating model defines the technical inspection, trust boundary, trust boundary, operating checks, and service path for this repository. System Architecture System architecture maps the runtime boundary, data/control flow, cloud or local deployment surface, and operating assumptions for this repository. Service Architecture Service architecture defines the cloud resources, account information, usage controls, and production guardrails needed to turn this repo into a scoped service without publishing public financial assumptions. baseline diffs, red-team"] end UI & CLI --> ING RL --> RBAC SAFE --> RAG LLM --> AUDIT & PROM & OTEL AUDIT --> SF & DB OTEL --> DD EVAL --> SF & DB style Governance fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style Observability fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32 style DataPlatform fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0 Loading Tech Stack Layer Technologies Backend API Python 3.11+, FastAPI, Uvicorn, Pydantic Frontend React 18, Vite Auth JWT (HS256) with key rotation, OIDC (RS256) via JWKS discovery RAG ChromaDB, deterministic hash embeddings, in-memory fallback LLM Providers OpenAI, Ollama, AWS Bedrock, stub (offline deterministic) Eval Custom harness -- accuracy, groundedness, helpfulness, safety scoring Storage SQLite, Chroma, JSONL event logs Data Platform Snowflake (eval + audit), Databricks (MLflow + Delta Lake) Observability Prometheus, Grafana, OpenTelemetry (OTLP), Datadog-ready Infrastructure Docker Compose, Kubernetes (HPA, TLS ingress, AlertManager), Terraform (AWS + GCP) CI/CD GitHub Actions (CI, security scan, Docker publish, Cloudflare Pages deploy) Security pip-audit, Bandit, Trivy container scanning Quick Start One-command demo (recommended) git clone https://github.com/KIM3310/enterprise-llm-adoption-kit.git cd enterprise-llm-adoption-kit make demo-local # auto-selects Ollama if available, otherwise stub Manual setup # 0. Backend requires Python 3.11+. # If your default python3 is older, pass a known interpreter: # make BOOTSTRAP_PYTHON=/path/to/python3.11 backend-install # 1. Backend make backend-install cd app/backend && .venv/bin/python -m app # starts on http://localhost:8000 # 2. Frontend (separate terminal) cd app/frontend npm install && npm run dev # starts on http://localhost:5173 # 3. Verify everything make verify # syntax, deps, pytest, smoke, frontend build Docker cd infra && docker-compose up --build # Backend: http://localhost:8000 Frontend: http://localhost:5173 Kubernetes kubectl create namespace llm-adoption kubectl apply -f infra/k8s/secret.yaml kubectl apply -f infra/k8s/ Key Capabilities Capability Description Code RBAC Role-to-access-group mapping enforced at RAG retrieval time (Employee / Ops / Admin) app/backend/app/rbac.py Prompt Injection Detection Keyword heuristics flag known injection patterns before LLM invocation app/backend/app/injection.py Safety Policy Engine 22 regex patterns targeting exfiltration, escalation, and adversarial prompts (ReDoS-safe) app/backend/app/safety.py PII Redaction Email, phone, and ID masking with per-category event tracking app/backend/app/redaction.py Audit Logging Structured JSON logs with SHA-256 hashing in enterprise mode and auto-retention pruning app/backend/app/audit.py RAG Retrieval ChromaDB + deterministic hash embeddings with RBAC-filtered document access app/backend/app/rag.py Eval Harness Accuracy, groundedness, helpfulness, safety scoring with baseline diffs and red-team datasets evals/runner/ LLMOps Metrics Request latency, token counts, usage tracking, policy events via Prometheus app/backend/app/metrics.py Circuit Breaker LLM provider failure isolation with configurable threshold and cooldown app/backend/app/llm_adapter.py Multi-provider LLM Hot-swappable runtime config across OpenAI, Ollama, Bedrock, and stub backends app/backend/app/llm_adapter.py Snowflake Integration Eval results and audit logs persisted to Snowflake (env-var gated) app/backend/app/snowflake_adapter.py Databricks Integration MLflow experiment tracking and Delta audit tables in Unity Catalog app/backend/app/databricks_adapter.py OpenTelemetry OTLP trace export with Datadog-ready integration app/backend/app/telemetry.py Core API Endpoint Description POST /auth/login Issue JWT (local_jwt or OIDC mode) POST /uc1/architecture LLM-assisted architecture query (RBAC-gated RAG) POST /uc2/log-intel Log intelligence and root cause generation GET /audit/summary Governance and audit log summary GET /metrics Prometheus metrics (requests, latency, tokens, usage, policy events) GET /health Runtime posture and diagnostics GET /ops/service-brief Operational service brief and readiness summary GET /ops/resource-pack Resource pack for architecture and evidence Snowflake / Databricks Integration Snowflake -- set SNOWFLAKE_ACCOUNT to activate. Stores eval results and audit logs; supports query_eval_history() , query_audit_history() , aggregate reporting. Databricks -- set DATABRICKS_HOST to activate. MLflow experiment tracking per eval run; Delta audit tables in Unity Catalog; databricks-cli or service-principal OAuth auth. See .env.example for the full configuration surface. Datadog-Ready Pack Datadog-ready resource pack: docs/datadog/README.md Existing env hooks already reserve a Datadog integration lane in .env.example Current state: asset sync and OTLP wiring are prepared, but live tenant integration is intentionally disabled by default Best use: show how enterprise LLM governance, audit, latency, and rollout readiness would be observed in one operator-facing Datadog surface Project Structure enterprise-llm-adoption-kit/ app/ backend/ # FastAPI backend (RBAC, RAG, safety, audit, LLM adapters) app/ main.py # FastAPI app with governance middleware rbac.py # Role-to-access-group mapping safety.py # 22-pattern safety policy engine injection.py # Prompt injection detection redaction.py # PII redaction engine audit.py # Structured audit logging with SHA-256 hashing auth.py # JWT/OIDC authentication llm_adapter.py # Multi-provider LLM router with circuit breaker rag.py # ChromaDB RAG with RBAC-filtered retrieval snowflake_adapter.py databricks_adapter.py metrics.py # Prometheus metric definitions telemetry.py # OpenTelemetry instrumentation config.py # 60+ env vars, type-safe parsing frontend/ # React + Vite UI evals/ runner/ # Eval harness (run_eval, eval_gate, baseline) datasets/ # Test datasets (initial, red-team, Korean) reports/ # Generated eval reports and diffs infra/ k8s/ # Kubernetes manifests (HPA, TLS, AlertManager) aws/terraform/ # AWS ECS + ALB Terraform module gcp/terraform/ # GCP Terraform module docker-compose.yml # Local multi-service orchestration monitoring/ # Grafana dashboard + AlertManager rules tests/ # 30+ test files, 84% backend coverage docs/ # Architecture, ops, blueprint, Datadog, evals docs scripts/ # Demo runners, quality gates, release ops Technical Proof Boundary Primary architecture lane: enterprise LLM governance, applied AI controls, and platform integration Strongest proof surface: governance pipeline, eval harness, observability surfaces, and deployment-ready backend/frontend split What is real here: RBAC, safety pipeline, audit logging, metrics, integration adapters, CI/CD, and deployment scaffolding What is bounded here: architecture cases and documents are synthetic, and Snowflake / Databricks / Bedrock integrations are env-gated Operating Commands make verify runs the backend syntax/dependency checks, Python suite, smoke diagnostics, and frontend production build. app/backend/.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests app/backend/tests -q exercises the backend services, adapters, runtime scorecards, and UI metadata contracts. cd app/frontend && pnpm build produces the static frontend bundle used by the Cloudflare Pages deployment path. CI/CD GHCR Docker image published on every push to main . Security scan (pip-audit, bandit, Trivy) runs on schedule. Frontend auto-deploys to Cloudflare Pages. Related Projects For governed NL-to-SQL analytics, see Nexus-Hive . For the data pipeline layer, see lakehouse-contract-lab . License MIT Cloud + AI Architecture This repository includes a neutral cloud and AI engineering blueprint that maps the current proof surface to runtime boundaries, data contracts, model-risk controls, deployment posture, and validation hooks. Cloud + AI architecture blueprint Machine-readable architecture manifest Validation command: python3 scripts/validate_architecture_blueprint.py Enterprise Productization Product operating model defines the technical inspection, trust boundary, trust boundary, operating checks, and service path for this repository. System Architecture System architecture maps the runtime boundary, data/control flow, cloud or local deployment surface, and operating assumptions for this repository. Service Architecture Service architecture defines the cloud resources, account information, usage controls, and production guardrails needed to turn this repo into a scoped service without publishing public financial assumptions. уЪЦТЃЁС║║тБФжђЈжю▓№╝їтюеућхтГљУАеТа╝тњїућхтГљжѓ«С╗Хт║ћућеСИГ№╝їуЏ«тЅЇТ»ЈтЉеТюЅТЋ░СИЄТЮА AI ТЈљуц║ућ▒тЙ«Уй»УЄфуаћуџё MAI ТеАтъІт«їТѕљ№╝їУђїТГцтЅЇ Excel тњї Outlook ТЏ┤СЙЮУхќ OpenAI тњї Anthropic уџёТеАтъІсђѓ

жџЈуЮђ AI у╝ќуеІтиЦтЁитй╗т║ЋТћ╣тЈўуеІт║ЈтЉўуџёТЌЦтИИтиЦСйюТхЂуеІ№╝їтиЦуеІтИѕуЙцСйЊу║иу║иУГдуц║РђюAI уќ▓ті│уЌЄРђЮТГБтюеУћЊт╗ХсђѓС╗іт╣┤ 2 Тюѕ№╝їуеІт║ЈтЉўТѓЅСИ╣уЅ╣ ┬и тЇАжЏитЈЉтИЃС║єСИђу»ЄуѕєуЂФТќЄуФа№╝їуЏ┤УеђУ┐ЎуДЇуќ▓ТЃФуюЪт«ътГўтюе№╝їтЇ┤тЙѕт░ЉТюЅС║║ТЈљУхисђѓ
тцџтљЇуеІт║ЈтЉўтљЉ Business Insider жђЈжю▓№╝їAI ТіђТю»У┐ГС╗БжђЪт║дтцфт┐Ф№╝їУЄфти▒тДІу╗ѕУ┐йУхХСИЇСИі№╝їУ┐ЏУђїС║ДућЪУЂїтю║ТЌатіЏТёЪ№╝їтЂџС║ІтЂюТ╗ъСИЇтЅЇсђѓ
С║║тиЦТЎ║УЃйТЌХС╗БУБ╣ТїЪуЮђСИђУѓАтєЁтЇиуІѓТй«№╝їуеІт║ЈтЉўС╗гУбФУ┐ФуєгтцюсђЂТЌаС╝ЉТГбтіауЈГсђѓУ┐ъу╗ГтѕЏСИџтѕЏСИџУђЁсђЂтЅЇтцќтЇќт╣│тЈ░ Postmates тЅ»Тђ╗УБЂТюг ┬и у┤бТќ»№╝їС╣ЪтюежюЇт░ћУїеуџётИќтГљУ»ёУ«║тї║жЂЊтЄ║С║єУЄфти▒уџёуёдУЎЉсђѓС╗ќтєЎжЂЊ№╝џРђютЊфТђЋтЈфС╝ЉТЂ»СИђт░ЈТЌХ№╝їжЃйТђ╗УДЅтЙЌУЄфти▒уЎйуЎйТхфУ┤╣С║єтцДжЄЈС║ДтЄ║ТЌХжЌ┤сђѓРђЮ
т╣┐тЉітБ░Тўј№╝џТќЄтєЁтљФТюЅуџёт»╣тцќУи│УйгжЊЙТјЦ№╝ѕтїЁТІгСИЇжЎљС║јУХЁжЊЙТјЦсђЂС║їу╗┤уаЂсђЂтЈБС╗цуГЅтйбт╝Ј№╝Ѕ№╝їућеС║јС╝ажђњТЏ┤тцџС┐АТЂ»№╝їУіѓуюЂућёжђЅТЌХжЌ┤№╝їу╗ЊТъюС╗ЁСЙЏтЈѓУђЃ№╝їITС╣Іт«ХТЅђТюЅТќЄуФатЮЄтїЁтљФТюгтБ░Тўјсђѓ

ТёЪУ░бITС╣Іт«ХуйЉтЈІ Уй»тфњТќ░тЈІ2199365 сђЂ HH_KK сђЂ тЈќС╗ђС╣ѕтљЇ сђЂ тЇјтЇЌтљ┤тйдуЦќ сђЂ УАЦУЇ»тљќ сђЂ уІгуФІТЉётй▒тИѕ сђЂ жбеУдІТџЅСИђ уџёу║┐у┤бТіЋжђњ№╝Ђ
ITС╣Іт«Х 7 Тюѕ 7 ТЌЦТХѕТЂ»№╝їТЇ«Уи»жђЈуцЙС╗іТЌЦТЈ┤т╝ЋСИЅСйЇуЪЦТЃЁС║║тБФТХѕТЂ»№╝їСИГтЏйС║║тиЦТЎ║УЃйтѕЮтѕЏтЁгтЈИ DeepSeek№╝ѕТи▒т║дТ▒ѓу┤б№╝ЅТГБтюеуаћтЈЉУЄфТюЅ AI Уі»уЅЄсђѓтдѓТъюТХѕТЂ»т▒ът«ъ№╝їТГцСИЙТюЅТюЏжЎЇСйјтЁХт»╣УІ▒С╝ЪУЙЙсђЂтЇјСИ║уџёСЙЮУхќсђѓ
уЪЦТЃЁС║║тБФжђЈжю▓№╝ї DeepSeek УЄфуаћуџё AI Уі»уЅЄСИ╗УдЂжЮбтљЉ AI Тјеуљєтю║ТЎ» сђѓТГцтцёуџёРђюТјеуљєРђЮТїЄуџёТў»тцДТеАтъІУ«Гу╗Ѓт«їТѕљтљј№╝їт║ћућетГдтѕ░уџёуЪЦУ»єтѕєТъљТќ░ТЋ░ТЇ«сђЂућЪТѕљтєЁт«╣сђѓ
тдѓТъюУ»ЦжА╣уЏ«ТѕљтіЪ№╝їжѓБС╣ѕ DeepSeek т░єУ┐ЏтєЏтЇіт»╝СйЊуаћтЈЉжбєтЪЪ№╝їт╣Ху╗ЎтЇјСИ║уГЅСИГтЏйуДЉТіђтиетц┤тИдТЮЦтЁеТќ░уФъС║ЅтјІтіЏсђѓ
Radio Free Mobile тѕєТъљтИѕуљєТЪЦтЙи ┬и ТИЕУјј№╝ѕITС╣Іт«ХТ│е№╝џRichard Windsor№╝ЅУАеуц║№╝џРђюУІ▒С╝ЪУЙЙтюеСИГтЏйуџётИѓтю║С╗йжбЮти▓тЪ║ТюгтйњжЏХ№╝їСИћуј░уіХС╗ЇС╝џТїЂу╗ГсђѓжЎцжЮъ DeepSeek УЃйУјитЙЌТюђтЁѕУ┐ЏуџётѕХжђатиЦУЅ║№╝їтљдтѕЎтЁХУі»уЅЄтЄаС╣јТ▓АТюЅжћђтЙђТхитцќтИѓтю║уџёТю║С╝џсђѓтЏаТГц№╝їУ┐ЎСИђСИЙТјфт╣ХСИЇС╝џт»╣УІ▒С╝ЪУЙЙжђаТѕљт«ъжЎЁтй▒тЊЇРђЮсђѓ
жЋ┐ТюЪС╗ЦТЮЦ№╝ї DeepSeek СИђуЏ┤С╗ЦТјетіе AI ТіђТю»уфЂуа┤УђїжЌ╗тљЇ №╝ї УђїжЮъСИЊТ│еС║јтЋєСИџтїќ сђѓТХѕТЂ»С║║тБФУАеуц║№╝їУ┐Ўт«ХтЁгтЈИуџёУі»уЅЄжА╣уЏ«С╗ЇтцёС║јТЌЕТюЪжўХТ«х№╝їуЏ«тЅЇТГБтюеСИјУі»уЅЄУ«ЙУ«АС╝ЂСИџсђЂТЎХтюєС╗БтиЦтјѓтњїтГўтѓетјѓтЋєт▒Ћт╝ђТ┤йУ░ѕсђѓтЈдСИђСйЇуЪЦТЃЁС║║тБФуД░№╝їDeepSeek уџёУі»уЅЄжА╣уЏ«тцДу║дтюеСИђт╣┤тЅЇтљ»тіесђѓ
ТГцтцќТХѕТЂ»С║║тБФжђЈжю▓№╝їDeepSeek ТюђУ┐ЉТЋ░ТюѕТїЂу╗ГтіауаЂТІЏУЂўУі»уЅЄУ«ЙУ«АтиЦуеІтИѕсђѓ СйєТІЏУЂўтйбт╝ЈУЙЃСИ║СйјУ░Ѓ №╝ї т╣ХТюфтюетЁгт╝ђт╣│тЈ░тЈЉтИЃУЂїСйЇС┐АТЂ» сђѓ
ућ▒С║јСИіУ┐░ТХѕТЂ»ТџѓТюфтЁгт╝ђ№╝їСИЅСйЇуЪЦТЃЁС║║тБФтЮЄУдЂТ▒ѓСИЇтЁитљЇсђѓТѕфУЄ│тЈЉуе┐№╝їDeepSeek ТџѓТюфтЏът║ћуй«У»ёУ»иТ▒ѓсђѓ
т╣┐тЉітБ░Тўј№╝џТќЄтєЁтљФТюЅуџёт»╣тцќУи│УйгжЊЙТјЦ№╝ѕтїЁТІгСИЇжЎљС║јУХЁжЊЙТјЦсђЂС║їу╗┤уаЂсђЂтЈБС╗цуГЅтйбт╝Ј№╝Ѕ№╝їућеС║јС╝ажђњТЏ┤тцџС┐АТЂ»№╝їУіѓуюЂућёжђЅТЌХжЌ┤№╝їу╗ЊТъюС╗ЁСЙЏтЈѓУђЃ№╝їITС╣Іт«ХТЅђТюЅТќЄуФатЮЄтїЁтљФТюгтБ░Тўјсђѓ

ITС╣Іт«ХТ│е№╝џтюеуЦъу╗ЈуДЉтГджбєтЪЪСИГ№╝їтЁет▒ђтиЦСйюуЕ║жЌ┤уљєУ«║У«цСИ║тцДжЄЈУёЉтєЁТ┤╗тіетцёС║јтљјтЈ░УЄфтіеУ┐љУАїуіХТђЂ№╝їтЈфТюЅУ┐ЏтЁЦтЈ»УбФт╣┐Т│ЏтЁ▒С║ФуџёРђютиЦСйюуЕ║жЌ┤РђЮуџёС┐АТЂ»№╝їТЅЇС╝џТѕљСИ║тЈ»ТЈЈУ┐░сђЂтЈ»С┐ЮТїЂсђЂтЈ»ТјеуљєуџётєЁт«╣сђѓУ»ЦуљєУ«║тИИућеС║јУДБжЄіТ│еТёЈсђЂТёЈУ»єУ«┐жЌ«тњїтцЇТЮѓТјеуљєСИГуџёС┐АТЂ»ТЋ┤тљѕУ┐ЄуеІсђѓ
тюеТюђТќ░У«║ТќЄСИГ№╝їAnthropic У«цСИ║ Claude ТеАтъІтєЁжЃеС╣ЪтГўтюеуЏИт║ћу╗ЊТъё№╝їС┐АТЂ»У┐ЏтЁЦСИђСИфтЈ»тљЉтЁеУёЉт╣┐ТњГуџёуЅ╣ТЮЃуЕ║жЌ┤тљј№╝їТЅЇС╝џтЈўтЙЌтЈ»УбФТюЅТёЈУ»єУ«┐жЌ«№╝їт«ўТќ╣т░єтЁХтЉйтљЇСИ║ J-spaceсђѓ
тюеУ«║ТќЄСИГ№╝їAnthropic У┐ўС╗Іу╗ЇС║єтљЇСИ║ J-lens№╝ѕтЁеуД░ Jacobian Lens№╝ЅтиЦтЁи№╝їУ┐ЎТў»СИђуДЇТЋ░тГдтѕєТъљТќ╣Т│Ћ№╝їућеС║јт╝▒тїќТеАтъІтљјтЈ░С╗╗тіАуџёт╣▓ТЅ░№╝їт╣ХУЂџуёд Claude тюеуЅ╣т«џТЌХтѕ╗тЁ│Т│еуџёТдѓт┐хсђѓ
СИ║жфїУ»Ђ J-space уџётГўтюе№╝їAnthropic тѕЌтЄ║С║є 4 жА╣УДѓт»Ъу╗ЊТъю№╝џ
тюеС╗╗ТёЈу╗Ўт«џТЌХжЌ┤тєЁ№╝їУ»бжЌ« Claude ТГБтюеТђЮУђЃС╗ђС╣ѕ№╝їуёХтљјСй┐уће J-lens ТЪЦуюІтйЊТЌХтЁХ J-space СИГтГўтюетЊфС║ЏРђюТдѓт┐хРђЮсђѓ
УдЂТ▒ѓ Claude уЅбУ«░тЁгт╣│№╝їуёХтљјУДѓт»Ът«ЃТюЅТёЈтю░т░єтЁгт╣│уџёТдѓт┐хТІќтѕ░ J-space№╝їС╗ЦтЈіТў»тљдУЃйТїЂС╣Ётїќсђѓ
тюеУДБтє│тЃЈтЏйжЎЁУ▒АТБІУ┐ЎТаитцЇТЮѓуџёжЌ«жбўТЌХ№╝їJ-space т░▒тЃЈСИђСИфУЇЅуе┐Тюг№╝їтйЊуаћуЕХС║║тЉўТћ╣тЈўтЁХтєЁт«╣ТЌХ№╝їтЁІті│тЙиуџёТюђу╗ѕУЙЊтЄ║С╣ЪС╝џжџЈС╣ІТћ╣тЈўсђѓ
тйЊСИђСИфТдѓт┐хУбФТІќтЁЦ J-space тљј№╝їClaude уџёС╗╗СйЋтЁХС╗ќжЃетѕє№╝їтЇ│Сй┐ТГБтюетЂџт«їтЁеСИЇтљїуџёС║ІТЃЁ№╝їС╗ЇуёХУЃйтцЪуюІтѕ░У»ЦТдѓт┐х№╝їуёХтљјтюеУЄфти▒уџёС╗╗тіАСИГтѕЕућет«Ѓсђѓ
Anthropic тюеУ«║ТќЄСИГУАеуц║№╝їУ┐ЎуДЇу╗ЊТъёт╣ХжЮъ Claude ТъХТъёСИГжбётЁѕУ«ЙУ«Ауџёу╗ёТѕљжЃетѕє№╝їУђїТў»тюеУ«Гу╗ЃУ┐ЄуеІСИГУЄфУАїтЄ║уј░№╝їРђюТјеТхІтјЪтЏаТў»У┐ЎуДЇТќ╣т╝ЈТюЅтіЕС║ју╗ёу╗ЄУ«Ау«ЌРђЮсђѓ
т«ўТќ╣У┐ўтђЪућеРђюУХІтљїТ╝ћтїќРђЮТдѓт┐хУ»┤ТўјУ┐ЎСИђуј░У▒А№╝їУ»ЦТдѓт┐хтјЪТюгт▒ъС║јућЪуЅЕтГдТдѓт┐х№╝їТїЄСИЇтљїуЅЕуДЇтюеуЏИС╝╝уј»тбЃтјІтіЏСИІ№╝їуІгуФІТ╝ћтїќтЄ║уЏИС╝╝уЅ╣тЙЂсђѓ
ТюгТќЄСИГ№╝їAnthropic тђЪућеУ»ЦТдѓт┐хТЈЈУ┐░С║║УёЉСИј AI ТеАтъІтЈ»УЃйтЏатцёуљєтцЇТЮѓС┐АТЂ»уџёТЋѕујЄжюђТ▒ѓ№╝їУђїтЄ║уј░тіЪУЃйСИіуЏИС╝╝уџёу╗ЊТъёУ«ЙУ«Асђѓ
т╣┐тЉітБ░Тўј№╝џТќЄтєЁтљФТюЅуџёт»╣тцќУи│УйгжЊЙТјЦ№╝ѕтїЁТІгСИЇжЎљС║јУХЁжЊЙТјЦсђЂС║їу╗┤уаЂсђЂтЈБС╗цуГЅтйбт╝Ј№╝Ѕ№╝їућеС║јС╝ажђњТЏ┤тцџС┐АТЂ»№╝їУіѓуюЂућёжђЅТЌХжЌ┤№╝їу╗ЊТъюС╗ЁСЙЏтЈѓУђЃ№╝їITС╣Іт«ХТЅђТюЅТќЄуФатЮЄтїЁтљФТюгтБ░Тўјсђѓ

title_original: Q1 2026 Innovation Graph update: Open source collaboration is accelerating worldwide
body_original:
Kevin Xu ┬и @khxu
July 7, 2026
4 minutes
Share:
Q1 2026 was a banner quarter for open source collaboration, as shown by the GitHub Innovation Graph’s economy collaborators metric, part of the latest data release. Outbound collaboration, defined as the sum of git pushes and pull requests sent from developers in one economy to public repositories in another economy, grew by 16% quarter-over-quarter from Q4 2025 to Q1 2026.
That’s the second highest quarter-over-quarter growth rate we’ve seen since 2020. The highest was in Q2 2020, with a 21% growth rate, when many of us suddenly and collectively decided to use our computers more .
In third place was Q1 2023, with a 9% growth rate. This was the first quarter after a research lab blogged about a new website they made, and they enticed users to sign up and file bug reports by offering a chance to win up to $500 in API credits . Evidently, sweepstakes are unreasonably effective motivators.
Metrics by economy
While it’s clear that collaboration is growing globally, plotting these and other metrics separately by economy highlights the different trajectories of the world’s developer communities:
Now, it’s your turn to analyze the underlying datasets yourself for interesting pursuits, such as improving how economic growth is measured or estimating the impact of a research lab’s new website . While we have no sweepstakes to offer (for now), we think there are fascinating data stories strewn throughout these CSVs just waiting to be told.
One example might be the impressive recent growth in Syria starting in Q4 2025, which coincides with changes we made to enable broader access to GitHub functionality following the relaxation of sanctions and export controls on the country :
We’re grateful to the developers who advocated for these changes and the communities that continue to help spread the word, such as GitSyria . Thanks to their efforts, we’re happy to share that we’ve been able to provide the GitHub Student Developer Pack to over 8,000 verified Syrian students in just the last six months.
Helping maintainers manage collaboration
While greater collaboration leads to many benefits, we recognize that the rapid increase in contribution volume has strained several communities. Ashley Wolf, our Director of Open Source Programs, wrote about the Eternal September of Open Source in February 2026, describing how maintainers are managing new contribution dynamics and what we’re doing to try to help. Features we’ve shipped include:
Pull request limits: You can set a maximum number of open pull requests that users without write access may have open in your repository at one time, giving you a more proactive way to manage contribution volume.
Repo-level pull request and issue controls : Gives maintainers the option to limit pull request and issue creation to collaborators or disable pull requests and issues entirely.
Pinned comments on issues : You can now pin a comment to the top of an issue from the comment menu.
Banners to reduce comment noise : Experience fewer unnecessary notifications with a banner that encourages people to react or subscribe instead of leaving noise like “+1” or “same here.”
Pull request performance improvements : Pull request diffs have been optimized for greater responsiveness and large pull requests in the new files changed experience respond up to 67% faster.
Faster issue navigation : Easier bug triage thanks to significantly improved speeds when browsing and navigating issues as a maintainer.
Temporary interaction limits : You can temporarily enforce a period of limited activity for certain users on a public repository.
Do you have feedback on the directions we’re exploring? Share it in the community discussion .
Share what is working for your projects, where the gaps are, and what would meaningfully improve your experience maintaining open source.

title_original: AI law startup Norm raises $120M, hits unicorn valuation | TechCrunch
body_original:
In Brief
Posted:
7:35 AM PDT ┬и July 7, 2026
Image Credits: akinbostanci (opens in a new window) / Getty Images
Dominic-Madori Davis
AI law startup Norm raises $120M, hits unicorn valuation
AI law startup Norm on Tuesday said it has raised $120 million in a Series C funding round led by Khosla Ventures, valuing the almost three-year-old startup at $1.2 billion.
Norm has built an AI-native law firm, called Norm Law, that uses the companyРђЎs own AI agents, employs human attorneys to supervise them, and offers legal services to enterprise clients. ItРђЎs also building AI agents that can supervise other AI agents as they go about their tasks.
The company charges based on outcomes rather than billing its clients hourly, in contrast to the rest of the industry.
Norm is one of the many legal AI startups that have popped up in the past few years, such as Harvey and Legora, looking to capitalize on the need to automate tedious work.
Other investors in NormРђЎs Series C include Bain, Craft Ventures, Coatue, Vanguard, New York Life, TIAA, Tony James (former president and COO of Blackstone), Jeff Hammes (former chairman of Kirkland & Ellis), and Fenwick LLP. The fresh capital will be used to help build out the product and hire more attorneys.
The company has raised more than $260 million in funding to date.

title_original: Introducing AI agents and tools in AWS Marketplace - AWS
body_original:
Introducing AI agents and tools in AWS Marketplace
Posted on:
Jul 16, 2025
AWS Marketplace now offers AI agents and tools from AWS Partners, allowing customers to find and buy third-party AI agent solutions with streamlined procurement and multiple deployment options. Customers can accelerate their discovery of AI agents and agent tools in a centralized catalog, while enjoying the benefits of purchasing through AWS Marketplace, and Partners can quickly bring their AI agent solutions to market.
Customers can explore AI agent products on the new РђюAI Agent & ToolsРђЮ solution page. Using natural language, customers can search and receive results that match their specific use cases. When evaluating solutions, customers can review listings that support model context protocol (MCP) and agent-to-agent (A2A) standard protocols, along with various deployment options, to determine the best-fit solution for their needs. Customers can then purchase and deploy their chosen solutions through various paths, including Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Runtime, or add tools to AgentCore Gateway to accelerate agent development.
For AWS Partners, AI Agents and Tools in AWS Marketplace accelerates customer reach and adoption for agentic solutions. By listing their AI agents and tools, Partners can leverage established AWS Marketplace channels to streamline sales, offer flexible pricing, and provide secure AWS deployment options. Partners can categorize their offerings and highlight MCP and A2A protocol support, enhancing discoverability through advanced search and filtering in the AWS Marketplace catalog. Integration with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore services further simplifies deployment for customers, reducing time to value and providing a secure, scalable environment for customers building innovative agentic solutions.
Start exploring AI agent solutions in AWS Marketplace . Learn how AWS Partners can start selling by accessing the AWS Marketplace Seller Guide .

title_original: Global Internal Automation Agents for a $50BN CPG - Eliza
body_original:
Global Internal Automation Agents for a $50BN CPG
Client: Global $50BN CPG
Time to First Value: 2 months
Users Enabled: 144,000 employees
Our Problem
Problem
The company operates at a scale few organizations match Рђћ 144,000+ employees across six global zones, a portfolio of hundreds of brands, and operations spanning supply chain, sales, marketing, finance, and HR in nearly every major market worldwide.
Inside that sprawling organization, thousands of manual, repetitive workflows consumed employee time every day: generating reports, reconciling data across systems, routing approvals, formatting documents, pulling information from disparate tools, and dozens of other tasks that followed predictable patterns but still required human effort to execute.
The challenges were structural:
Fragmented processes across zones. The same workflow Рђћ say, generating a weekly sales summary or processing a vendor change request Рђћ might be handled differently in Middle Americas, Europe, Africa, and Asia Pacific. No single automation could simply be "turned on" globally.
Volume that overwhelmed point solutions. At 144K employees, even a workflow that saved 10 minutes per person per week translated to tens of thousands of hours. But individual automation tools couldn't scale across the diversity of languages, systems, and business rules in play.
High bar for reliability. In a company where a missed report or a delayed approval could ripple across a supply chain serving billions of consumers, any automation had to be rigorously evaluated before deployment Рђћ not just functional, but accurate, consistent, and auditable.
The company didn't need a chatbot. They needed a production-grade automation platform that could be built once, evaluated rigorously, and deployed globally Рђћ zone by zone Рђћ without breaking.
Our Solution
Solution
We partnered with the company to build Nexus , a global internal automation platform powered by OpenAI models and designed for enterprise-scale deployment across all six of the company's operating zones.
Agentic automation framework: Nexus is built on a modular agent architecture, where each automation is a discrete, testable unit Рђћ with defined inputs, outputs, business rules, and escalation paths. This modularity enables the team to build automations once and adapt them per zone with localized parameters (language, systems, compliance rules) rather than rebuilding from scratch.
10+ production automations across core functions The initial rollout covers high-frequency, high-impact workflows across multiple departments:
Finance: Automated report generation, variance analysis summaries, and budget reconciliation workflows that previously required hours of manual spreadsheet work.
Supply Chain: Demand signal aggregation and exception alerting, pulling data from multiple source systems into actionable summaries for planners.
Sales & Commercial: Territory performance summaries, promotional effectiveness reports, and customer data enrichment Рђћ generated on-demand instead of manually assembled.
HR & Operations: Employee onboarding checklist automation, policy Q&A, and approval routing that previously required multi-step email chains.
Each automation follows a standard lifecycle: discovery Рєњ build Рєњ eval Рєњ pilot Рєњ production rollout.
Rigorous evaluation pipeline: Before any automation reaches production, it passes through a structured evaluation framework Рђћ measuring accuracy, latency, edge-case handling, and output quality against human baselines. Evals run continuously, not just at launch, ensuring automations maintain quality as underlying models and data change.
Zone-by-zone global rollout: Nexus launched into production in Middle Americas in March 2026 Рђћ the first of six global zones. The rollout strategy is deliberate: prove value and reliability in one zone, incorporate learnings, then expand. Each subsequent zone inherits the core automation library and evaluation framework, with zone-specific adaptations for language, regulatory requirements, and local system integrations.
How it fits: Nexus integrates with the company's existing enterprise systems Рђћ SAP, Salesforce, internal data lakes, and collaboration tools Рђћ so employees interact with automations inside the workflows they already use. No new apps to install, no separate portals. The goal is invisible infrastructure: automations that just work.
Our Results
Results
March 2026: Production rollout to Middle Americas Рђћ the first of six global zones.
As of March 2026, Nexus is live in production in Middle Americas Рђћ the first of six global zones Рђћ with 10+ automations built across Finance, Supply Chain, Sales, and HR & Ops. The target user base is 144,000 employees globally, with the evaluation framework actively running continuous evals against OpenAI models to maintain quality as the platform scales. Global expansion is underway, with zone-by-zone rollout planned through 2026.
What's already changed:
Hours reclaimed at scale. Even in the first zone, automations are eliminating repetitive manual work across finance, supply chain, and commercial teams Рђћ freeing employees to focus on higher-judgment activities.
Standardization across a complex org. For the first time, the company has a single automation platform and evaluation framework that works across zones, languages, and business units Рђћ creating a shared foundation for AI-powered operations globally.
A repeatable deployment playbook. The zone-by-zone rollout model Рђћ build Рєњ eval Рєњ pilot Рєњ expand Рђћ gives the organization a proven, low-risk path to scaling automations to all 144K employees without the disruption of a big-bang launch.

Problem
The company operates at a scale few organizations match Рђћ 144,000+ employees across six global zones, a portfolio of hundreds of brands, and operations spanning supply chain, sales, marketing, finance, and HR in nearly every major market worldwide.

Fragmented processes across zones. The same workflow Рђћ say, generating a weekly sales summary or processing a vendor change request Рђћ might be handled differently in Middle Americas, Europe, Africa, and Asia Pacific. No single automation could simply be "turned on" globally.
Volume that overwhelmed point solutions. At 144K employees, even a workflow that saved 10 minutes per person per week translated to tens of thousands of hours. But individual automation tools couldn't scale across the diversity of languages, systems, and business rules in play.
High bar for reliability. In a company where a missed report or a delayed approval could ripple across a supply chain serving billions of consumers, any automation had to be rigorously evaluated before deployment Рђћ not just functional, but accurate, consistent, and auditable.
The company didn't need a chatbot. They needed a production-grade automation platform that could be built once, evaluated rigorously, and deployed globally Рђћ zone by zone Рђћ without breaking.
