title_original: Meet the former Apple designer building a new AI interface at Hark
body_original:
Image Credits: Hark
AI
Meet the former Apple designer building a new AI interface at Hark
Tim Fernholz
9:00 AM PDT ┬и March 24, 2026
A secretive AI lab founded by serial entrepreneur Brett Adcock shared new details about what it believes is a novel marriage of model-building and hardware design that will change how humans interact with intelligent software.
The company said in a statement it would design multimodal end-to-end models, its hardware, and its interfaces in tandem to deliver a Рђюseamless end-to-end personal intelligence product.РђЮ The system will have a persistent memory of your life and can listen, see, and interact with the world in real time.
How that will be executed remains unclear outside the company, but HarkРђЎs ambition is representative of Silicon ValleyРђЎs ongoing hunt for the killer app that will make AI a desired consumer product, not features kludged dubiously into existing digital platforms.
РђюMy view is simple: todayРђЎs AI models arenРђЎt nearly intelligent enough, they feel quite dumb, and the devices we use to access them are fundamentally pre-AI,РђЮ Adcock wrote in a January internal memo shared with TechCrunch. РђюWeРђЎre moving toward a world that looks more like sci-fi characters Jarvis or Her, with systems that anticipate, adapt, and genuinely care about the people using them.РђЮ
Details are intentionally sparse, but Hark points to Director of Design Abidur Chowdhury as a key hire. Previously an industrial designer at Apple credited with leading the design team behind the iPhone Air and other recent models, London-born Chowdhury left last fall after meeting with Adcock and buying into his vision for updating the way humans automate their lives.
In an exclusive interview with TechCrunch, Chowdhury declined repeated invitations to spill the beans on HarkРђЎs roadmap, only saying that the public can anticipate a first release of the companyРђЎs AI models this summer. Asked about different approaches to working and living alongside AI, the designer did offer a few clues.
Loading the playerРђд
РђюWhat was very clear for me at the time is that the world is clearly changing, but weРђЎre using the same devices Рђд everythingРђЎs been designed around these existing platforms,РђЮ Chowdhury told. РђюVery few people are really going after what the future is. ThereРђЎs so much that we could be doing if intelligence was at the base layer of everything we touched instead of becoming an app or a website at that upper layer.РђЮ
Chowdhury points to the awkwardness of everyday tasks of filling out forms, sharing information between devices, or the mundane tasks of booking travel or planning home renovation.
РђюThose are entire evenings of time where I have to plan Рђд the anxiety of, you know, I spend my workday thinking about this in the back of my head, oh, I have to do this,РђЮ Chowdhury said. РђюWe genuinely believe that all of the small tasks that pile up to be kind of gargantuan things today can be sort of automated from our lives.РђЮ
Chowdhury says the company knows what it is building, but canРђЎt yet say how users will experience it. His comments suggest that wearables, like MetaРђЎs glasses, seem unlikely.
РђюIРђЎm not the biggest believer in a lot of the wearable AI platforms that people are talking about right now,РђЮ Chowdhury said. РђюI donРђЎt think itРђЎs appropriate to put a layer between humanity and the interfaces we use in the world. I have similar discomfort with pins, or that kind of stuff that is going around with cameras.РђЮ
When generative AI first arrived on the scene, Chowdhury at first saw it as a flash in the pan, but successive generations of models convinced him that it would change his work. Hark, the word, means to pay attention, which Chowdhury says offers a thoughtful framing for the companyРђЎs mission.
РђюTraditional user experience always is about finding the simplest thing for everyone,РђЮ he told TechCrunch. РђюThe future user experience will be finding the right thing for each individual. And I believe that can happen. But it requires a lot of work.РђЮ
The focus on elegance and simplicity for users echoes the high points of AppleРђЎs product design, and naturally brings to mind Jony Ive, the legendary former Apple designer who is now developing AI native-hardware at OpenAI. A comparison that a HarkРђЎs spokesperson declined to explore.
Another parallel that comes to mind is how Elon MuskРђЎs xAIРђЎs work on advanced models dovetails with TeslaРђЎs work on autonomous vehicles and humanoid robots.
There is similar corporate synergy between AdcockРђЎs humanoid robotics company Figure and the new AI labs. HarkРђЎs models are already being trained on FigureРђЎs robots, although it is not clear to what end. A person familiar with the companiesРђЎ plans says there is no intention to combine them.
Hark employs 45 engineers and designers, including former Meta AI researchers and designers from Apple and Tesla, all of whom are working on the same campus that hosts AdcockРђЎs other companies. Hark expects to begin using a new cluster of thousands of Nvidia GPUs in April.
Now Hark, backed by $100 million in personal seed money from Adcock, will join the scramble for talent as the worldРђЎs biggest companies try to figure out the format that brings deep learning models into daily life Рђћ and at a time when frustration with the existing models for digital life is hitting a fever pitch.
РђюIt just feels like thereРђЎs an opportunity for better, and IРђЎve not felt like that since the iPhone came up,РђЮ Chowdhury said.
Topics
AI , Exclusive , figure , hark
When you purchase through links in our articles, we may earn a small commission . This doesnРђЎt affect our editorial independence.
Tim Fernholz
Senior Reporter
Tim Fernholz is a journalist who writes about technology, finance and public policy. He has closely covered the rise of the private space industry and is the author of Rocket Billionaires: Elon Musk, Jeff Bezos and the New Space Race. Formerly, he was a senior reporter at Quartz, the global business news site, for more than a decade, and began his career as a political reporter in Washington, D.C.
You can contact or verify outreach from Tim by emailing tim.fernholz@techcrunch.com or via an encrypted message to tim_fernholz.21 on Signal.

title_original: The AI services transformation may be harder than VCs think
body_original:
Image Credits: Bryce Durbin / TechCrunch
AI
The AI services transformation may be harder than VCs think
Connie Loizos
6:20 PM PDT ┬и September 28, 2025
Venture capitalists have convinced themselves theyРђЎve found the next big investing edge: using AI to wring software-like margins out of traditionally labor-intensive services businesses. The strategy involves acquiring mature professional services firms, implementing AI to automate tasks, then using the improved cash flow to roll up more companies.
Leading the charge is General Catalyst (GC), which has dedicated $1.5 billion of its latest fundraise to what it calls a РђюcreationРђЮ strategy thatРђЎs focused on incubating AI-native software companies in specific verticals, then using those companies as acquisition vehicles to buy established firms Рђћ and their customers Рђћ in the same sectors. GC has placed bets across seven industries, from legal services to IT management, with plans to expand to up to 20 sectors altogether.
РђюServices globally is a $16 trillion revenue a year globally,РђЮ said Marc Bhargava , who leads GCРђЎs related efforts, in a recent interview with TechCrunch . РђюIn comparison, software is only $1 trillion globally,РђЮ he noted, adding that the allure of software investing has always been its higher margins. РђюAs you get software to scale, thereРђЎs very little marginal cost and thereРђЎs a great deal of marginal revenue.РђЮ
If you can automate services business, too, he said Рђћ tackling 30% to 50% of those companies with AI, and even automating up to 70% of those core tasks in the case of call centers Рђћ the math begins to look irresistible.
The game plan seems to be working right now. Take Titan MSP , one of GCРђЎs portfolio companies. The investment firm provided $74 million over two tranches to help the company develop AI tools for managed service providers, then it acquired RFA, a well-known IT services firm. Through pilot programs, says Bhargava, Titan demonstrated it could automate 38% of typical MSP tasks. The company now plans to use its improved margins to acquire additional MSPs in a classic roll-up strategy.
Similarly, the firm incubated Eudia , which focuses on in-house legal departments rather than law firms. Eudia has signed up Fortune 100 clients including Cargill, Del Monte, and Stripe, offering fixed-fee legal services powered by AI rather than traditional hourly billing. The company recently acquired Johnson Hana, an alternative legal service provider, to expand its reach.
GC looks to double Рђћ at least Рђћ the EBITDA margin of those companies that itРђЎs acquiring, Bhargava explained.
The powerhouse firm isnРђЎt alone in its thinking. The venture firm Mayfield has carved out $100 million specifically for РђюAI teammatesРђЮ investments, including Gruve, an IT consulting startup that acquired a $5 million security consulting company, then grew it to $15 million in revenue within six months while achieving an 80% gross margin, according to its founders.
РђюIf 80% of the work will be done by AI, it can have an 80% to 90% gross margin,РђЮ Navin Chaddha, MayfieldРђЎs managing director, told TechCrunch this summer . РђюYou could have blended margins of 60% to 70% and produce 20% to 30% net income.РђЮ
Solo investor Elad Gil has been pursuing a similar strategy for three years, backing companies that acquire mature businesses and transform them with AI. РђюIf you own the asset, you can [transform it] much more rapidly than if youРђЎre just selling software as a vendor,РђЮ Gil said in an interview with TechCrunch this spring .
But early warning signs suggest this whole services-industry metamorphosis may be more complicated than VCs anticipate. A recent study by researchers at Stanford Social Media Lab and BetterUp Labs that surveyed 1,150 full-time employees across industries found that 40% of those employees are having to shoulder more work because of what the researchers call РђюworkslopРђЮ Рђћ AI-generated work that appears polished but lacks substance, creating more work (and headaches) for colleagues.
The trend is taking a toll on the organizations. Employees involved in the survey say theyРђЎre spending an average of nearly two hours dealing with each instance of workslop, including to first decipher it, then decide whether or not to send it back, and oftentimes just to fix it themselves.
Based on those participantsРђЎ estimates of time spent, along with their self-reported salaries, the authors of the survey estimate that workslop carries an invisible tax of $186 per month per person. РђюFor an organization of 10,000 workers, given the estimated prevalence of workslop . . . this yields over $9 million per year in lost productivity ,РђЮ they write in a new Harvard Business Review article.
Bhargava disputed the notion that AI is overhyped, arguing instead that all these implementation failures actually validate GCРђЎs approach. РђюI think it kind of shows the opportunity, which is, itРђЎs not easy to apply AI technology to these businesses,РђЮ he said. РђюIf all the Fortune 100 and all these folks could just bring in a consulting firm, slap on some AI, get a contract with OpenAI, and transform their business, then obviously our thesis [would be] a little bit less robust. But the reality is, itРђЎs really hard to transform a company with AI.РђЮ
He pointed to the technical sophistication required in AI as the most critical missing puzzle piece. РђюThereРђЎs a lot of different technology. ItРђЎs good at different things,РђЮ he said. РђюYou really need these applied AI engineers from places like Rippling and Ramp and Figma and Scale, who have worked with the different models, understand their nuances, understand which ones are good for what, understand how to wrap it in software.РђЮ That complexity is exactly why GCРђЎs strategy of pairing AI specialists with industry experts to build companies from the ground up makes sense, he argued.
Still, thereРђЎs no denying that workslop threatens to undermine Рђћ to some extent Рђћ the strategyРђЎs core economics. Even if a holding company is created as a starting point, if the acquired companies reduce staff as the AI efficiency thesis suggests they should, theyРђЎll have fewer people available to catch and correct AI-generated errors. If the companies maintain current staffing levels to handle the additional work created by problematic AI output, the huge margin gains that VCs are counting on might never be realized.
Arguably, these scenarios should perhaps slow the scaling plans that are central to the VCsРђЎ roll-up strategies and that potentially undermine the numbers that make these deals attractive to them. But letРђЎs face it; it will take more than a study or two to slow down most Silicon Valley investors.
In fact, because they typically acquire businesses with existing cash flow, GC says its Рђюcreation strategyРђЮ companies are already profitable Рђћ a marked departure from the traditional VC playbook of backing high-growth, cash-burning startups. ItРђЎs also likely a welcome change for the limited partners behind venture firms, who have bankrolled years of losses at companies that never reached profitability.
РђюAs long as AI technology continues to improve, and we see this massive investment and improvement in the models,РђЮ Bhargava said, РђюI think thereРђЎll just be more and more industries for us to help incubate companies.РђЮ
Topics
AI , TC , Venture Capitalists
When you purchase through links in our articles, we may earn a small commission . This doesnРђЎt affect our editorial independence.
Connie Loizos
Editor in Chief & General Manager
Loizos has been reporting on Silicon Valley since the late РђЎ90s, when she joined the original Red Herring magazine. Previously the Silicon Valley Editor of TechCrunch, she was named Editor in Chief and General Manager of TechCrunch in September 2023. SheРђЎs also the founder of StrictlyVC, a daily e-newsletter and lecture series acquired by Yahoo in August 2023 and now operated as a sub brand of TechCrunch.

AI
The AI services transformation may be harder than VCs think
Connie Loizos
6:20 PM PDT ┬и September 28, 2025
Venture capitalists have convinced themselves theyРђЎve found the next big investing edge: using AI to wring software-like margins out of traditionally labor-intensive services businesses. The strategy involves acquiring mature professional services firms, implementing AI to automate tasks, then using the improved cash flow to roll up more companies.
Leading the charge is General Catalyst (GC), which has dedicated $1.5 billion of its latest fundraise to what it calls a РђюcreationРђЮ strategy thatРђЎs focused on incubating AI-native software companies in specific verticals, then using those companies as acquisition vehicles to buy established firms Рђћ and their customers Рђћ in the same sectors. GC has placed bets across seven industries, from legal services to IT management, with plans to expand to up to 20 sectors altogether.
РђюServices globally is a $16 trillion revenue a year globally,РђЮ said Marc Bhargava , who leads GCРђЎs related efforts, in a recent interview with TechCrunch . РђюIn comparison, software is only $1 trillion globally,РђЮ he noted, adding that the allure of software investing has always been its higher margins. РђюAs you get software to scale, thereРђЎs very little marginal cost and thereРђЎs a great deal of marginal revenue.РђЮ
If you can automate services business, too, he said Рђћ tackling 30% to 50% of those companies with AI, and even automating up to 70% of those core tasks in the case of call centers Рђћ the math begins to look irresistible.
The game plan seems to be working right now. Take Titan MSP , one of GCРђЎs portfolio companies. The investment firm provided $74 million over two tranches to help the company develop AI tools for managed service providers, then it acquired RFA, a well-known IT services firm. Through pilot programs, says Bhargava, Titan demonstrated it could automate 38% of typical MSP tasks. The company now plans to use its improved margins to acquire additional MSPs in a classic roll-up strategy.
Similarly, the firm incubated Eudia , which focuses on in-house legal departments rather than law firms. Eudia has signed up Fortune 100 clients including Cargill, Del Monte, and Stripe, offering fixed-fee legal services powered by AI rather than traditional hourly billing. The company recently acquired Johnson Hana, an alternative legal service provider, to expand its reach.
GC looks to double Рђћ at least Рђћ the EBITDA margin of those companies that itРђЎs acquiring, Bhargava explained.
The powerhouse firm isnРђЎt alone in its thinking. The venture firm Mayfield has carved out $100 million specifically for РђюAI teammatesРђЮ investments, including Gruve, an IT consulting startup that acquired a $5 million security consulting company, then grew it to $15 million in revenue within six months while achieving an 80% gross margin, according to its founders.
РђюIf 80% of the work will be done by AI, it can have an 80% to 90% gross margin,РђЮ Navin Chaddha, MayfieldРђЎs managing director, told TechCrunch this summer . РђюYou could have blended margins of 60% to 70% and produce 20% to 30% net income.РђЮ
Solo investor Elad Gil has been pursuing a similar strategy for three years, backing companies that acquire mature businesses and transform them with AI. РђюIf you own the asset, you can [transform it] much more rapidly than if youРђЎre just selling software as a vendor,РђЮ Gil said in an interview with TechCrunch this spring .
But early warning signs suggest this whole services-industry metamorphosis may be more complicated than VCs anticipate. A recent study by researchers at Stanford Social Media Lab and BetterUp Labs that surveyed 1,150 full-time employees across industries found that 40% of those employees are having to shoulder more work because of what the researchers call РђюworkslopРђЮ Рђћ AI-generated work that appears polished but lacks substance, creating more work (and headaches) for colleagues.
The trend is taking a toll on the organizations. Employees involved in the survey say theyРђЎre spending an average of nearly two hours dealing with each instance of workslop, including to first decipher it, then decide whether or not to send it back, and oftentimes just to fix it themselves.
Based on those participantsРђЎ estimates of time spent, along with their self-reported salaries, the authors of the survey estimate that workslop carries an invisible tax of $186 per month per person. РђюFor an organization of 10,000 workers, given the estimated prevalence of workslop . . . this yields over $9 million per year in lost productivity ,РђЮ they write in a new Harvard Business Review article.
Bhargava disputed the notion that AI is overhyped, arguing instead that all these implementation failures actually validate GCРђЎs approach. РђюI think it kind of shows the opportunity, which is, itРђЎs not easy to apply AI technology to these businesses,РђЮ he said. РђюIf all the Fortune 100 and all these folks could just bring in a consulting firm, slap on some AI, get a contract with OpenAI, and transform their business, then obviously our thesis [would be] a little bit less robust. But the reality is, itРђЎs really hard to transform a company with AI.РђЮ
He pointed to the technical sophistication required in AI as the most critical missing puzzle piece. РђюThereРђЎs a lot of different technology. ItРђЎs good at different things,РђЮ he said. РђюYou really need these applied AI engineers from places like Rippling and Ramp and Figma and Scale, who have worked with the different models, understand their nuances, understand which ones are good for what, understand how to wrap it in software.РђЮ That complexity is exactly why GCРђЎs strategy of pairing AI specialists with industry experts to build companies from the ground up makes sense, he argued.
Still, thereРђЎs no denying that workslop threatens to undermine Рђћ to some extent Рђћ the strategyРђЎs core economics. Even if a holding company is created as a starting point, if the acquired companies reduce staff as the AI efficiency thesis suggests they should, theyРђЎll have fewer people available to catch and correct AI-generated errors. If the companies maintain current staffing levels to handle the additional work created by problematic AI output, the huge margin gains that VCs are counting on might never be realized.
Arguably, these scenarios should perhaps slow the scaling plans that are central to the VCsРђЎ roll-up strategies and that potentially undermine the numbers that make these deals attractive to them. But letРђЎs face it; it will take more than a study or two to slow down most Silicon Valley investors.
In fact, because they typically acquire businesses with existing cash flow, GC says its Рђюcreation strategyРђЮ companies are already profitable Рђћ a marked departure from the traditional VC playbook of backing high-growth, cash-burning startups. ItРђЎs also likely a welcome change for the limited partners behind venture firms, who have bankrolled years of losses at companies that never reached profitability.
РђюAs long as AI technology continues to improve, and we see this massive investment and improvement in the models,РђЮ Bhargava said, РђюI think thereРђЎll just be more and more industries for us to help incubate companies.РђЮ

SigmanticAI - The Cursor for Hardware Design
Suite of AI Agents and Tools to Accelerate HDL (Hardware Description Language) Design
Tamzid Razzaque
SigmanticAI
12 months ago
Embed
https://www.sigmanticai.com #ai #b2b #hardware #hard_tech #ai_assistant
Hey YC! We're Tamzid and Rohil from SigmanticAI .
The Problem with Creating Custom Hardware
HDL tooling hasnРђЎt changed much since the '90s: still slow, manual, and fragmented
Debugging eats up majority of hardware dev time
There's no seamless AI-native experience for building and reasoning about HDL
Our Solution
SigmanticAI . What it can do:
Natural language to synthesizable Verilog + testbenches
RL-driven debugging loop
Built-in onboarding , codebase annotation , and spec documentation generation
LLM-powered on both cloud and fully air-gapped/on-prem deployments
Seameless integration with VScode + your existing synthesis tools from Cadence, Synopsys, etc.
WeРђЎve already made a RISCV CPU start to finish with it, onboarded our first paying customer, and are getting 90%+ accuracy on the VerilogEval benchmark (NVIDIA framework)
Demo video
https://youtu.be/Cq3hpMflIA8
Asks!
We want to talk to:
Anyone working with FPGAs, custom chips, ML accelerators, or RISC-V cores
Anyone working in EDA
Anyone working with fine tuned LLMs for hardware
Try us out! Thank you!
https://www.sigmanticai.com
Who We Are
WeРђЎve spent years working on both LLMs and hardware. After working at Apple (Tamzid) and Amazon (Rohil) and research in FPGA mapping and LLaMA fine-tuning, we realized the real bottleneck in hardware design is tooling. WeРђЎve built custom CPUs, worked with EDA stacks, and experienced the pain firsthand. Conversations with engineers at Northrop Grumman, SciEngines, and Quadric made it clear: hardware desperately needs a smarter workflow.
See All Launches Рђ║

тЁХСИГСИђтљЇТХѕТЂ»С║║тБФжђЈжю▓№╝їТюгТгАС╗БуаЂТЅФТЈЈтиЦСйюућ▒ CISA ТЌЌСИІТћ╗тЄ╗жЮбУ»ёС╝░т░Ју╗ёУ┤ЪУ┤БсђѓУ»ЦтЏбжўЪжџХт▒ъС║ј CISA№╝їСИЊжЌеСИ║тљёТћ┐т║южЃежЌет╝ђт▒ЋТЋ░тГЌт«ЅтЁеУ»ёС╝░СИјТеАТІЪТћ╗жў▓Т╝ћу╗Ѓсђѓ

ITС╣Іт«ХТ│еТёЈтѕ░№╝їти▓уДўт»єжђњС║цуЙјтЏйждќТгАтЁгт╝ђтІЪУѓАућ│У»иуџё Anthropic№╝їтњїуЙјтЏйТћ┐т║ютЁ│у│╗тЄау╗ЈТ│бТіўсђѓС╗іт╣┤ 2 Тюѕ№╝їУ┐Ўт«ХТђ╗жЃеСйЇС║јТЌДжЄЉт▒▒уџёС╝ЂСИџТІњу╗ЮуД╗жЎц AI тєЁуй«жў▓ТіцТю║тѕХ№╝їУ»ЦТю║тѕХтЈ»жў▓ТГбтЁХС║║тиЦТЎ║УЃйТіђТю»УбФућеС║јУЄфСИ╗ТГдтЎесђЂтЏйтєЁуЏЉТјД№╝їтЈїТќ╣тЁ│у│╗УиїУЄ│тє░уѓ╣сђѓуЙјтЏйтЏйжў▓жЃежџЈтЇ│т»╣тЁХСИІУЙЙТГБт╝ЈСЙЏт║ћжЊЙжБјжЎЕУ«цт«џ№╝їУ┐ЎСИђТаЄУ»єС╗ЦтЙђС╗ЁућеС║јТХЅтФїтЇЈтіЕтбЃтцќжЌ┤У░ЇТ┤╗тіеуџётцќтЏйС╝ЂСИџсђѓ

body_original: Meta тюе Meta AI т║ћућеСИГТјетЄ║ Muse Image№╝їУ┐ЎТў» MSL ждќСИфтЏЙтЃЈућЪТѕљТеАтъІсђѓУ»ЦТеАтъІтљїТЌХуЎ╗жЎє WhatsApp тњї Instagram№╝їтљју╗Гт░єТЅЕт▒ЋУЄ│ Messenger тњї FacebookсђѓMuse Image тѕЕућежФўу║ДТјеуљєуљєУДБтцЇТЮѓТЈљуц║№╝їУЃйт░єтцџт╝ауЁДуЅЄТЌау╝ЮУъЇтљѕућЪТѕљжФўУ┤ежЄЈтЏЙтЃЈ№╝їТћ»ТїЂжбёУ«ЙсђЂтюеТЈљуц║СИГт╝ЋућетЏЙуЅЄС╗ЦтЈіуЏ┤ТјЦу╝ќУЙЉсђѓу╗Њтљѕт╝ЋућеТјеТќЄ№╝їMuse Image тЁитцЄТЎ║УЃйСйЊуЅ╣ТђД№╝џСИј Muse Spark жЁЇт»╣№╝їтЁѕТјеуљєућеТѕиТЈљуц║сђЂТљюу┤буйЉу╗ют╣ХУДётѕњ№╝їтєЇућЪТѕљтЏЙтЃЈ№╝їТЌетюеждќТгАт░ЮУ»ЋтЇ│тЙЌтѕ░ућеТѕиТюЪТюЏу╗ЊТъюсђѓ

title_original: Meta Superintelligence Labs ТјетЄ║ Muse Image тњї Muse Video
title_zh: Meta Superintelligence Labs ТјетЄ║ Muse Image тњї Muse Video
body_original: Meta Superintelligence Labs тЈЉтИЃждќСИфтфњСйЊућЪТѕљТеАтъІ Muse Image тњї Muse VideoсђѓMuse Image Тў»уЏ«тЅЇТюђтЁѕУ┐ЏуџётЏЙтЃЈућЪТѕљТеАтъІ№╝їУЃйу▓ЙуА«жЂхтЙфТїЄС╗цсђЂу▓ЙтЄєу╝ќУЙЉсђЂтцџтЈѓУђЃТъётЏЙ№╝їт╣ХтѕЕуће Instagram уцЙС║цСИіСИІТќЄсђѓт«ЃУ┐ўтЁитцЄТЎ║УЃйСйЊтиЦтЁиСй┐ућеУЃйтіЏт╣ХжЏєТѕљ Muse SparkсђѓућеТѕитЈ»жђџУ┐Є Meta AI т║ћућесђЂуйЉжАхсђЂInstagram Stories тњї WhatsApp У»Ћуће№╝їтѕЮтДІжЎљС║јжЃетѕєтЏйт«ХсђѓMuse Video тЪ║С║јуЏИтљїжбёУ«Гу╗ЃтЪ║уАђ№╝їт«ъуј░жФўУДєУДЅС┐ЮуюЪт║дт╣ХтјЪућЪТћ»ТїЂжЪ│жбЉсђѓ

title_original: Hugging Face Storage ТѕљСИ║ SkyPilot СИђу║ДтљјуФ»№╝џжЏХтЄ║уФЎУ┤╣УиеС║ЉтГўтѓе
title_zh: Hugging Face Storage ТѕљСИ║ SkyPilot СИђу║ДтљјуФ»№╝џжЏХтЄ║уФЎУ┤╣УиеС║ЉтГўтѓе
body_original: Hugging Face Storage уј░СИ║ SkyPilot уџёСИђу║ДтљјуФ»сђѓућеТѕижђџУ┐Є `hf://` URL тњїуј░ТюЅ HFTOKEN тЇ│тЈ»т░є Hugging Face Bucket№╝ѕУ»╗тєЎ№╝ЅТѕќТеАтъІ/ТЋ░ТЇ«жЏє/Space С╗Њт║Њ№╝ѕтЈфУ»╗№╝ЅТїѓУййтѕ░ SkyPilot С╗╗тіАСИГ№╝їТћ»ТїЂ MOUNT№╝ѕFUSE ТЄњтіаУйй№╝ЅТѕќ COPY ТеАт╝ЈсђѓSkyPilot тЈ»т░єС╗╗тіАУ░Ѓт║дтѕ░ 20+ С║ЉсђЂKubernetesсђЂSlurm тЈіТюгтю░жЏєуЙцуџёС╗╗ТёЈтЈ»уће GPU СИісђѓHugging Face СИЇТћХтЈќтЄ║уФЎтЈі CDN У┤╣уће№╝їТЋЁУиеС║ЉУ»╗тЈќТЋ░ТЇ«ТЌажбЮтцќТѕљТюгсђѓтГўтѓеС╗иТа╝ $12РђЊ18/TB/Тюѕ№╝їСйјС║ј AWS S3 тіатЄ║уФЎУ┤╣сђѓBucket тЪ║С║ј Xet№╝їтбъжЄЈТБђТЪЦуѓ╣тњїТеАтъІтЈўСйЊС╗ЁтГўтѓетњїС╝аУЙЊТћ╣Рђд

body_original: ТюгТгАТЏ┤Тќ░Тќ░тбъуЎ╗тйЋУ┐ЄТюЪУГдтЉітњїТЅІтіеТЮЃжЎљТеАт╝ЈТаЄУ«░№╝їт╣Хт░єжЎётіатиЦСйюуЏ«тйЋтіатЁЦ MCP roots/listсђѓС┐«тцЇС║є macOS СИІтЏатєЁтГўТБђТхІУ»»ТіЦт»╝УЄ┤тљјтЈ░С╝џУ»ЮтЇАжА┐ 15РђЊ20 уДњ№╝ѕтЏътйњУЄф 2.1.196№╝ЅсђЂтљјтЈ░С╝џУ»ЮтЏа token У┐ЄТюЪТ░ИС╣ЁСИЇтЈ»уће№╝ѕуј░УЄфтіеТЂбтцЇ№╝ЅсђЂС║цС║њт╝ЈС╝џУ»ЮСИГСИіСИІТќЄТїЄуц║тЎеТ»ЈУй«жЄЇтѕєТъљТЋ┤СИфт»╣У»Ют»╝УЄ┤ CPU тњїтєЁтГўтЏътйњуГЅжЌ«жбўсђѓтљїТЌХТћ╣У┐ЏС║єжЋ┐тЊЇт║ћТхЂТЌХуџёт«ъТЌХжбёУДѕТђДУЃй№╝їт╣ХжЎЇСйјС║єтГљС╗БуљєжЄЇТќ░тДћТЅўС╗╗тіАуџётђЙтљЉсђѓС║їУ┐ЏтѕХСйЊуД»тЄЈт░Љу║д 7 MB№╝їтљ»тіетєЁтГўтЄЈт░Љу║д 7 MBсђѓтидСЙДу«Гтц┤СИЇтєЇтЁ│жЌГтљјтЈ░С╗╗тіА/ти«т╝ѓ/тиЦСйюТхЂУ»дТЃЁУДєтЏЙ№╝їТћ╣СИ║ Escсђѓ

title_zh: тюе Claude Code СИГжђЅТІЕ Claude ТеАтъІСИјтіфтіЏу║ДтѕФ
body_original: Claude Code ТЈљСЙЏТеАтъІ№╝ѕmodel№╝ЅтњїтіфтіЏу║ДтѕФ№╝ѕeffort№╝ЅСИцуДЇУ«Йуй«сђѓТеАтъІжђЅТІЕтє│т«џУЃйтіЏУїЃтЏ┤№╝їТЏ┤тцДТеАтъІ№╝ѕтдѓ Claude Fable 5№╝ЅтюетЪ║тЄєСИіС╝ўС║ј Claude SonnetсђѓтіфтіЏу║ДтѕФСИЇтЇЋТў»ТђЮУђЃТЌХжЌ┤№╝їУ┐ўТјДтѕХУ»╗тЈќТќЄС╗ХТЋ░сђЂжфїУ»ЂТГЦжфцтЈітцџТГЦС╗╗тіАуџёТјеУ┐ЏТи▒т║дсђѓУЙЃжФўтіфтіЏСИІ Claude С╝џУЄфУАїУ»╗тЈќТќЄС╗ХсђЂУ┐љУАїТхІУ»Ћт╣ХтцЇТаИ№╝ЏУЙЃСйјтіфтіЏтѕЎТЏ┤тђЙтљЉУ»иТ▒ѓућеТѕиТЈљСЙЏСИіСИІТќЄсђѓУІЦ Claude ти▓ТјїТЈАСИіСИІТќЄСйєС╗ЇтЄ║жћЎ№╝їт║ћТЇбТЏ┤т╝║тцДТеАтъІ№╝ЏУІЦтЏаУи│У»╗ТќЄС╗ХТѕќТюфУ┐љУАїТхІУ»ЋУђїтЄ║жћЎ№╝їтѕЎт║ћТЈљжФўтіфтіЏу║ДтѕФсђѓ
